instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who discovers the sun leaves a small warmth inside anyone it touches at dawn, and who secretly carries that warmth to the coldest, darkest corners of her village.
tale:
Once upon a time, a girl named Lira discovered that the sun left a
small warm seed inside anyone it touched for the first time at dawn.
She began waking before everyone else and carrying these seeds in her
pockets to the dark corners of her village where light never reached.
She pressed one into the hand of the old man who had not smiled in
years, and one into the soil beneath the coldest doorstep, and one
against the window of the child who was afraid of the dark.
By summer the whole village glowed faintly from the inside and no one
knew exactly why, only that mornings felt worth waking up for.
And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who can hold sunlight in her cupped hands and shares it daily with a blind woman who has not felt warmth in years, in exchange for learning the names of birds by their song.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Ara found that she could hold sunlight in her cupped hands the way other children held water. She carried it to the blind woman at the edge of the village who had not felt warmth on her face in seven years. The woman lifted her head and said: that is exactly what I remembered. Ara came every morning after that and the woman taught her, in exchange, the names of every bird by its song alone. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a baker who keeps working through a morning when the sun forgets to rise, and whose fresh bread draws the frightened town together until dawn finally arrives.
tale: Once upon a time, the sun forgot to rise one morning and the world waited in darkness, uncertain what to do. A baker named Pol kept working because dough does not wait for light, and the smell of fresh bread drifted through the dark streets and drew people out of their houses. They gathered in the bakery and talked until dawn finally came, late and apologetic, and found that something had been begun in the dark that would not have started in the light. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who collects and presses shadows the sun leaves behind, keeping them like flowers in a book, until in old age he opens it and briefly sees them rise again.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Cass collected shadows that the sun left behind when it moved on. He kept them pressed between the pages of a thick book the way others kept flowers, and each shadow held the shape of whatever had cast it at the exact moment the sun had found it most beautiful. When he was old he opened the book and the shadows rose from the pages and stood briefly in their old shapes around him before dissolving into the afternoon light. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a village where the sun sets in the wrong direction, and a cartographer sent to correct the maps who instead stays for forty years, unable to leave the mystery unsolved.
tale: Once upon a time, there was a village where the sun rose from the east like everywhere else but set in the north, which confused travellers and delighted the villagers who had grown up expecting the unexpected. A cartographer came to correct the maps and stayed for forty years because she could not bear to leave a place where something as large as the sun did not behave as predicted. She never did correct the maps. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a sunflower that grows taller than a house and watches a girl through her window every morning, becoming her silent, faithful companion.
tale: Once upon a time, a sunflower grew taller than the house it stood beside and bent its great face to look in through an upstairs window. The girl who slept in that room woke each morning to find the flower watching her with its enormous dark eye and began to tell it her worries before breakfast. The sunflower could not answer but it turned to follow her when she crossed the garden and she understood that being faithfully watched was its own kind of answer. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a baker who learns to bake bread that tastes of the specific hour it was made, and who can sense exactly which loaf each customer needs.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman named Sol learned to bake bread that tasted of the specific hour she had made it — morning bread tasted of beginnings, noon bread of fullness, evening bread of the particular satisfaction of work completed. She opened a bakery and people came not for hunger but for the feeling they needed most, and she learned to know without asking which hour each loaf should be. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a day when the sun rises twice by mistake, and how everything it passes over the second time briefly looks like the most beautiful morning it has ever shown.
tale: Once upon a time, the sun made a mistake and rose twice in one day — once at dawn as usual and once again in the middle of the afternoon when it had circled back to check on something it had missed. Nobody was certain what it had forgotten but everything it passed over the second time looked briefly the way it had looked on the most beautiful morning of its life. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who is afraid of the dark and stays awake every night to watch over it, eventually becoming a great astronomer through sheer sustained attention rather than cleverness.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Fen was afraid of the dark and so she stayed awake every night to keep watch over the sun's absence. She became the best astronomer her country had ever produced, not because she was clever but because she looked at the night sky with the full attention of someone who could not afford to stop. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a painter who can only work in direct sunlight and moves her studio outdoors through every season, discovering that difficulty teaches her to see more clearly than comfort ever did.
tale: Once upon a time, there was a painter who could only work in direct sunlight and so she moved her studio to a hillside and worked through every season in rain and wind and cold, and the paintings she made in difficult light were better than the ones she had made in comfortable studios because difficulty had taught her to look harder. She gave her last painting to the hillside and left the frame nailed to the air. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a child who asks the sun why it is so far away, and the wisdom the sun's answer teaches her about closeness and distance for the rest of her life.
tale: Once upon a time, a child asked the sun why it was so far away and the sun said: I am exactly as far away as I need to be to warm you without burning you. The child thought about this for many years and applied the principle to every relationship she formed and was known throughout her life for knowing precisely how close to stand. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a man who spends thirty years walking west to stay in the exact hour of afternoon light his late wife had loved most.
tale: Once upon a time, a man named Oran spent thirty years walking west to stay in the same hour of afternoon light because it was the hour his wife had loved best. He circled the world four times and returned finally to stand in his own garden where she had planted three apple trees that were now enormous, their shadows crossing the grass in the exact late afternoon angle he had been chasing. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about the sun giving its last light each evening to the horizon as a gift of gratitude, and the debt only the two of them remember.
tale: Once upon a time, the sun gave its last light each day to the horizon as a gift for having held it all day without complaint. The horizon kept these gifts in long strips of colour — orange and rose and the deep burning red that appears just before dark — and on the evenings when the colours were most extraordinary the sun was paying a particular debt of gratitude that only the horizon and the sun remembered. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl born at exactly noon who grows up understanding she arrived at the day's fullest point, and who is incapable of doing anything by half.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Luz was born at exactly noon and grew up understanding that she had arrived at the day's fullest point, which meant she was constitutionally unable to do things by half. She loved completely, worked completely, and grieved completely, and when she was happy the people around her felt warm without knowing why. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a lighthouse keeper who comes to think of the sunrise as a colleague doing the same work from the opposite side of the water.
tale: Once upon a time, there was a lighthouse keeper who noticed that the sun and the lighthouse were doing the same work from opposite sides of the water, and he began to think of them as colleagues. He waved to the sunrise each morning and the sunrise, being the sunrise, offered nothing back except light, which he decided was more than most colleagues offered anyway. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a kingdom where the sun appears only three days a year, and a foreign visitor who realizes she has never once stopped to appreciate sunlight because she always had it.
tale: Once upon a time, a kingdom was so cold that the sun only appeared three days a year, and on those three days everyone stopped what they were doing and stood outside and tilted their faces up and were still. A foreign visitor asked what they were doing and was told: we are remembering. The visitor who came from a sunny land realised she had never once stopped to remember sunlight because she had never had to. She went home changed. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who learns to speak to the sun using a piece of polished copper, and becomes a healer who asks it which remedies each season will need.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl discovered she could speak to the sun by reflecting its light back at it using a piece of polished copper her grandmother had given her. The sun's replies came as warmth — gentle for yes, brilliant for you understand, cool for ask me again tomorrow. She grew up to be a healer and used the copper to ask the sun which remedies to prepare each season, and the sun was never wrong about what the coming months would need. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a farmer who plants his fields by the quality of the morning light rather than the calendar, and whose harvests are mysteriously always extraordinary.
tale: Once upon a time, there was a farmer who planted his fields by the quality of the morning light rather than the calendar, and his harvests were always extraordinary while his neighbours' were merely good. They asked him for his secret and he said: I plant when the light looks like it means it. Nobody knew what this meant but they noticed he was always right. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy too afraid to go outside, for whom the sun comes in through every window of his house instead, until he follows its light all the way to the garden gate.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Sol was afraid to go outside, so the sun came to him through every window of his house and arranged itself in patches on the floor that he could step in or around depending on how brave he felt that day. Over months the patches moved and he followed them and one morning he noticed he had followed a patch of sun all the way to the garden gate and was standing in the open air. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a clockmaker who builds a clock that runs only on sunlight, useless for telling time but perfect for measuring days worth living.
tale: Once upon a time, a clockmaker built a clock that ran on sunlight alone and refused to work on cloudy days, which made it useless for practical timekeeping but perfect for measuring days worth living. People brought it out on fine mornings and watched it run and felt grateful. On grey days they kept it wrapped in cloth and remembered. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who wakes before dawn every day for thirty years to watch the sunrise, and what she learns from describing each one differently.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Helia woke before dawn every day to watch the sun arrive and noticed that it came differently each morning — cautious in autumn, bold in summer, tender in early spring. She wrote down a description of every sunrise for thirty years and when she was old she read them back and found she had written, without knowing it, a complete account of how to approach each day of the year. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about the sun and the moon trading places for a single day and discovering, to their surprise, that each had been doing the other's job slightly wrong all along.
tale: Once upon a time, the sun and the moon agreed to trade places for one day and discovered that each had been doing the other's work incorrectly for centuries. The sun spent the night being too bright and waking everyone up. The moon spent the day being too dim and worrying the farmers. They returned to their proper positions with a new respect that expressed itself as the particularly warm afternoon light on autumn days and the especially clear moonlight on summer nights. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woman who keeps a jar of the best morning light of her life sealed on her kitchen shelf, and what happens when she finally gives it away.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman kept a jar of morning light on her kitchen shelf. She had collected it on the best morning of her life and sealed it carefully and looked at it when she needed reminding. On the day she gave it away to her daughter she opened it first and found it smelled of bread and wood smoke and the specific quality of air after rain, and her daughter said: that is exactly what our house smells like. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who spends years trying to prove that the sun rises equally for everyone, and the unexpected proof he finds in who chooses to rise and meet it.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy was told that the sun rose for everyone equally and he spent a long time trying to verify this before concluding that while the sun rose equally, not everyone rose to meet it, and the ones who did seemed warmer on the inside than those who didn't, which was its own kind of proof. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a desert kingdom whose people, having no shade, grow up entirely unafraid of light, and what visitors from shadowed countries learn from them.
tale: Once upon a time, a desert kingdom had no shade and so its people learned to carry light easily — they were not afraid of it or dazzled by it. When travellers came from shadowed countries they found the desert people honest and direct in the way of those who have nothing to hide in. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who chases the last light of every evening because she cannot bear for good days to end, and how far that chasing eventually takes her.
tale: Once upon a time, there was a girl who chased the last light of every evening because she could not bear for good days to end. She became a traveller who was always moving west and eventually a sailor who had extended her best summer day to six months by following the sun across every ocean. When she finally stopped she was very old and very brown and she watched a sunset without moving and found that it was enough. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a glassblower whose workshop window throws shifting patterns of afternoon sun, and how she arranges her work to match the changing seasons of light.
tale: Once upon a time, a glassblower made a window that caught the afternoon sun and threw it across her workshop in a pattern that changed with the seasons, and she arranged her work to match the pattern — delicate things in the gentle light of spring, bold things in the blazing light of summer. People who bought her glass said it held a warmth inside that no other glass had. She did not explain why. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a single golden feather the sun leaves behind during an eclipse, and the quiet protection it offers the girl who finds and keeps it.
tale: Once upon a time, the sun left a single golden feather on the ground where it had rested during a solar eclipse, and a girl found it and kept it in her pocket. The feather was warm in cold weather and gave off a faint light in the dark. She never spoke about it but people who walked beside her on difficult paths said they had felt, for no reason they could name, that things would be all right. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a composer who writes a symphony that can only be properly heard in full sunlight, because she has woven the light itself into the music.
tale: Once upon a time, a composer wrote a symphony that could only be heard properly in full sunlight because she had woven the light itself into the music — the instruments' tones shifted with the sun's movement and the piece was never exactly the same twice. Concert halls tried to replicate it under lamps but something was always missing. The only authentic performances were in open fields at noon. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a monk who walks east every morning to watch the sunrise in complete silence, and the one thing fifty years of the practice finally teaches him.
tale: Once upon a time, a monk walked east every morning to face the sunrise and spent the hour it took for the sun to fully clear the horizon in complete silence. He had been doing this for fifty years and when he was asked what he had learned he said: I have learned that the sun rises whether or not I am there to see it, and that I am there to see it. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woman who carries a single wooden door from her childhood home wherever she travels, leaning it against any wall to let people walk through into the warmth of where she grew up.
tale:
Once upon a time, a woman named Dara carried her home on her back —
not a shell like a snail's, but a small wooden door she had unhinged
from her childhood house before it was torn down.
Wherever she travelled she leaned the door against any wall and walked
through it, and on the other side was always the same smell: bread
and cedar and the particular quiet of a Sunday afternoon.
People who saw her do this thought she was strange, until the day
one of them was far from home and frightened, and Dara held the door
open and said simply: you can come through too.
After that there was always a small queue on difficult evenings of
people waiting to walk through a door that led nowhere but felt like
everything.
And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who discovers her house breathes differently at night, slower and deeper, and spends a summer listening to it settle through the dark hours.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Mara discovered that her house breathed differently at night — slower, deeper — and she spent a summer listening to it settle and creak and sigh its way through the dark hours and felt, for the first time, that she lived inside something alive that was also keeping her. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a man who builds a house so exactly suited to his own proportions that guests find it strange and he finds it restoring.
tale: Once upon a time, a man built a house so exactly suited to his own proportions — shelves at his precise reach, ceilings just high enough for his comfort, windows placed where his eyes naturally fell — that guests found it slightly uncomfortable and he found it more restoring than any other place on earth. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woman who moves twelve times in twenty years and always hangs the same three things in the same arrangement, knowing a house becomes home the moment the last one is placed.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman named Hesta moved twelve times in twenty years and in each house she hung the same three things in the same configuration and the house became home the moment the third thing was on the wall, which was always a drawing her daughter had made at age four and which was not beautiful but was hers. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who finds a door in his neighbourhood that isn't attached to any building, and what its existence changes about how he sees his own front door.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy found a door in his neighbourhood that he had walked past a thousand times and had never noticed was not attached to a building. It stood in a gap between two houses, solid and painted red, and opened onto the alley behind it. He knocked. No one answered. He went home and found his own door looked different after that: chosen, not accidental, standing for something. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woman who plants climbing roses on every wall of her house until it disappears behind flowers each summer, insisting that the house is simply what the roses grow on.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman planted climbing roses on every wall of her house until the house in summer disappeared behind flowers and guests said: I can't see the house. She said: that is the point. The house is what the roses grow on. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woman who keeps every key she has ever owned on one heavy ring, each one a record of a place she was once let in.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Thea kept all the keys she had ever owned — to flats and houses and sheds and padlocks — on a ring that grew heavier every year. She said the ring was a map of every place she had been let in, every threshold she had been trusted with, and she counted the keys when she felt she did not belong anywhere. She always found she had more keys than she remembered. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a man who spends thirty years building a house by hand, one stone at a time, guided only by a sketch he keeps folded in his shirt pocket.
tale: Once upon a time, a man spent thirty years building a house by hand, one stone at a time, without a contractor or a plan beyond the sketch he kept in his shirt pocket. The house was irregular and warm and full of small decisions that accumulated into something no architect could have designed because they required thirty years of learning what the builder needed from a wall. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woman who lives in a lighthouse and understands that a home can be a warning and a welcome at exactly the same time.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman named Calla lived in a lighthouse and understood that a home could be a warning and a welcome simultaneously, that the same light that said stay away from the rocks to ships said come here, there is a person, you are not alone. She never confused the two functions and she kept the light burning. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy whose grandmother's house smells of a specific, irreplaceable combination of cedar, bread, and old books, and his lifelong attempt to recreate it.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy discovered that his grandmother's house smelled of a specific combination of cedar, bread, and old books and that no other place in the world smelled exactly that way and that this specific smell was home in a way that no single address would ever be. He spent his adult life trying to recreate it and failed, which he did not mind, because the trying kept him paying attention to the smell of every place he lived. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who learns that the word for home means something slightly different in every language she studies, and what the smallness of her own language's word for it teaches her.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Nora realised that the word home had a different meaning in every language she learned and that the English word was unusually small — one syllable, four letters — for something so large, and that the smallness of the word was perhaps the point: home is the thing so necessary it needs almost no sounds to say. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woman who designs a house meant to be dismantled and rebuilt elsewhere, numbering every stone, and what she learns from moving it three times in her life.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman built a house that was designed to be dismantled and rebuilt elsewhere, with numbered stones and fitted joints and a handbook for reconstruction. She moved it three times in her life and each time found that the house was the same in every country it stood in and that its sameness in different landscapes was the most honest definition of home she had found: portable, consistent, yours. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a man returning home after ten years away, finding his house cared for by strangers in his absence, and the week he spends putting small things back exactly where they belong.
tale: Once upon a time, a man named Fen came home after ten years away and found his house had been well cared for by the family who had rented it and that everything was clean and orderly and none of it was his. He spent a week putting things back — the particular angle of a chair, the specific pile of books beside the bed — and felt himself returning not just to a place but to a self that had been stored there waiting. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who keeps a guest book asking every overnight visitor to write one thing the house gave them, and what she learns reading it back after twenty years.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl kept a guest book in which every visitor who stayed the night was asked to write one thing the house had given them. After twenty years the book was full of things she had not known the house could give: courage, the first full sleep in months, the specific permission of a quiet Sunday morning, the feeling that they were expected. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woman who lives in a house built by her great-great-grandmother and what she finds hidden in the walls during a renovation.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman named Bree lived in a house that had been built by her great-great-grandmother and found, in the walls during a renovation, a cache of letters, tools, and a single dried flower that the original builder had placed there during construction — not hidden, simply stored, the way you store things you trust the future with. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who grows up in a house on wheels, learning to make a new home in thirty-six different places before he turns eighteen.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy grew up in a house on wheels and learned to make home in thirty-six different places before he was eighteen and found that home was not a location but a practice: the specific things you did in a new place to tell your body this is safe, this is yours, this is where you sleep. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woman who draws floor plans from memory of every house she has ever lived in, and the pattern she discovers in what she always unconsciously chooses.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman named Willa drew a floor plan of every house she had ever lived in from memory and found, laying them side by side, that she had always chosen houses with a particular relationship between the kitchen and the garden — close, with a window between them — and that this preference, repeated across twenty years and seven houses, was a more accurate record of who she was than anything she had written about herself. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a man with no fixed address who carries a small box of objects from every home he has ever had, arranging them wherever he arrives to make that place briefly his.
tale: Once upon a time, a man who had no fixed address kept a small box of objects — a stone from his mother's garden, a door key from his first flat, a piece of wood from the floor of his childhood home — and arranged them on whatever surface was available when he arrived somewhere new, and called the arrangement home, because home was what you brought with you, and he had learned to carry it light. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who discovers a stone cellar wall in her house, older than any record of it, that stays warm to the touch through every winter.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Sola discovered that the oldest part of her house — a stone wall in the cellar, older than any record of it — was still warm to the touch in winter. The warmth came from deep in the earth and had been radiating through that wall for two centuries of winters and she pressed her hands to it on the coldest days and felt the unbroken continuity of that warmth and understood that some things kept giving long after the giving was remembered. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woman who builds her house facing west against everyone's advice, because she wants to watch endings rather than beginnings from her front door.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman built her house facing west rather than south, which all her neighbours advised against, because she had decided that endings — the last light, the closing of the day — were the thing she most wanted to face from home, and she watched the sunset from her front door every evening for forty years and felt she had made the right orientation for herself. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who finds a list in his late father's handwriting titled 'things the house needs', and what reading it teaches him about a parent he barely knew.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy found a list in his father's handwriting tucked into a book: things the house needs. The list was from twenty years before he was born. Several things on the list had never been fixed and several had been fixed in ways that left their traces visible, and reading it he felt he knew his father in the house — worried about it, attentive to it, imperfect in caring for it, trying — and the list taught him more about love as a practice than anything his father had said aloud. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woman who spends a winter cataloguing every sound her house makes, until she can identify any room in it with her eyes closed.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman named Iris spent a winter cataloguing every sound her house made — the furnace's specific rhythm, the pipe in the third-floor bathroom that knocked at temperature changes, the way the front door's seal sounded different in rain — and by spring had a complete acoustic portrait of her home that she could hear, with her eyes closed, from any room in it, and she found the familiarity of those sounds the most private form of belonging she had. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who discovers her house has three different names — its legal address, its builder's name for it, and its local nickname — and decides to use all three.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl discovered that her house had three addresses — its current legal address, the name it had been given by its original builder, and the local name it had acquired over generations — and she began using all three interchangeably and found that having multiple names for the same place made the place feel larger, more storied, more itself. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a man who retires and, for the first time, learns what his house looks like in the middle of an ordinary weekday afternoon.
tale: Once upon a time, a man retired and for the first time had no commute and spent a week learning his house in the middle of the day, in the hours he had never been there. He found it was a different house in the afternoon — quieter, lighter in specific rooms, the garden more visible. He rearranged his chair to face the afternoon window and spent fifteen years in the house he had lived in for twenty without fully knowing. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woman who insists no guest may leave her house without eating something, however small, and what the village remembers about her after sixty years of this rule.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman named Oma made a rule that no guest could leave her house without eating something, however small, and over sixty years she fed thousands of people at her door and her table and her kitchen standing up, and when she died the church was full of people who had eaten a biscuit or a bowl of soup in her house on a hard day, and they were full of something that a biscuit is not, and they knew what it was and said it. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who builds a treehouse not for childhood reasons but because she believes a home above the ground is a different, more honest relationship with the earth.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl built a house in a tree not for childhood reasons but because she had decided that a home above the ground was a different relationship with the earth than a home on it — less heavy, less permanent, more honest about the fact that all tenure is temporary and all belonging is an agreement with the particular branch you are resting on. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a man who keeps a fire burning in his hearth from the first cold day of autumn to the last cold day of spring, exactly as his father and grandfather did before him.
tale: Once upon a time, a man named Wick kept a fire burning in his hearth from the first cold day of autumn to the last cold day of spring for forty years without letting it go out, because his grandfather had kept the same fire and his father had kept the same fire and the continuity of the flame was the family's home in a way that the building around it only partially was. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woman who moves into a house empty for eight years and spends her first month simply listening to what it has forgotten before adding anything of her own.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman moved into a house that had been empty for eight years and spent the first month listening to what the house had forgotten — the sounds that empty houses lose, the particular quality of warmth that bodies add to rooms — before she began adding her own things to it. She said the house had needed to remember it was a house before it could become a home. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who learns that her great-grandmother's word for home also meant the place where you are forgiven, and what she tries to build from that definition.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Cora learned that the word for home in her great-grandmother's language also meant the place where you eat and the place where you sleep and the place where you are forgiven, all in one word, and she tried for the rest of her life to live in places that were all three simultaneously. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a man who keeps every paint sample he has ever considered for his walls, hundreds of small rectangles that together record every time he thought about changing something.
tale: Once upon a time, a man kept all the paint samples he had ever considered for his walls — hundreds of small rectangles of colour, each labelled with the room and year it was considered and whether it had been used — and the collection was a record of every time he had thought about changing something and either done it or decided it was fine as it was, and both types of records were equally honest. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy told he is ordinary, who discovers that everyone he loves has also been called ordinary by someone else, and what he comes to believe the word actually means.
tale:
Once upon a time, a boy named Coll was told he was ordinary, and he
spent a long time believing it until he noticed that every person he
had ever loved was also called ordinary by someone else.
He began to keep a notebook of ordinary people — the woman who
remembered every customer's name, the man who swept the same square
of street every morning with complete attention, the child who laughed
at everything honestly.
By the time the notebook was full he understood that ordinary was not
a description of a person but a failure of observation on the part of
the describer.
He gave the notebook to the person who had called him ordinary first,
and that person read it and was quiet for a long time and then said
thank you in a way that meant something more than the words.
And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who remembers the exact face of every person she has ever met, and the portrait painter she becomes in order to lighten that weight.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Cass discovered that she could remember the exact face of every person she had ever met, not as a gift but as a weight — the accumulated faces of everyone she had loved, lost, or briefly passed on a busy street all present simultaneously behind her eyes. She became a portrait painter and found that externalising the faces onto canvas lightened the interior archive, one face at a time. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about the smallest boy in his school, and the specific, unexpected advantages his size gives him once he stops believing they don't matter.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Oren was the smallest person in his school and spent years being told this would matter less as he grew and found, when he stopped growing, that it had never mattered in the ways he had feared and had mattered in ways no one had thought to mention: he was fast, he fit in small spaces, he was overlooked by people who underestimated him, and the underestimation was the most useful tool he ever had. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woman who has the gift of making every person she speaks with feel like the most interesting person she has met that week.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman named Dara had the unusual quality of making every person she spoke with feel that they were the most interesting person she had encountered that week. This was not flattery but attention, and attention, given fully and without agenda, is the rarest and most accurate form of respect. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a father who tells his daughter the most important thing a person can do is be exactly one consistent self, and what she learns trying to live up to it.
tale: Once upon a time, a father told his daughter that the most important thing a person could do was to be exactly one person consistently — not the same in all circumstances but recognisably themselves in all of them. She spent thirty years trying to be this and found it harder than any skill she had learned and more worth the effort. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a child who cannot remember faces but remembers every voice perfectly, and how she navigates the world by sound alone.
tale: Once upon a time, a child named Brim could not remember faces but remembered voices perfectly — their pitch, their rhythm, the specific music of how a particular person said a particular word — and she navigated the world by sound and was never lost in a crowd and never failed to recognise a friend. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a man told his whole life he is ordinary, who spends his life proving it true by doing ordinary things with such complete care that they become extraordinary.
tale: Once upon a time, a man spent his life being told he was ordinary and spent his life proving this correct by doing ordinary things with such complete attention and such consistent care that the ordinary things became extraordinary by accumulation, and people who knew him said he was the finest person they had ever met, which surprised him. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl raised to believe every person she meets knows something she doesn't, and the vast, unofficial education she assembles by always looking for it.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Lise was raised by her grandmother to believe that every person she met knew at least one thing she did not, and she spent her life confirming this to be true and finding the one thing in each person and learning it and carrying it forward, and by the time she was old she carried in her the knowledge of thousands of people and was the most generally educated person in her region without having attended any institution. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who discovers he is the fourth generation to carry the same family name, and how he learns to carry the weight of it without being defined by it.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy discovered that he was the fourth generation in his family to carry the same name and felt the weight of it — the names before him, the lives they had lived, the expectations embedded in the letters — and spent years either growing into it or running from it before understanding that a name was not a destiny but a starting place, and he could choose what to make of what he had been given. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woman who becomes a nurse because being with people at their most vulnerable feels like the closest thing to true knowledge of another person.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman named Sol became a nurse because she found that being with people in their most vulnerable moments was the closest thing to true knowledge of a person that professional life offered, and she spent forty years knowing people at their most honest and found the knowledge the most humbling and the most sustaining work available. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who grows up in a village where everyone knows everyone's business, moves away for anonymity, and discovers what she actually wanted instead.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl grew up in a village where everyone knew everyone else's business and found this suffocating until she moved to a city where no one knew anyone and found that anonymity was not freedom but a different kind of confinement, and she moved back to the village and made peace with being known by accepting that being known required being knowable and being knowable required being honest, and the honesty was the freedom she had been looking for all along. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a man told he has no talent who believes it for twenty years, until he finds the one specific thing he was always good at.
tale: Once upon a time, a man named Wick was told he had no talent and believed it for twenty years until he found the specific thing he was talented at — which was not the things he had been tested on — and the twenty years of believing the wrong thing about himself were neither wasted nor good but were simply the path, and the path had taken him where the path took him and now he was there. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a child who learns the name of every person in her town and makes a game of greeting each one, and the reputation that quiet habit eventually earns her.
tale: Once upon a time, a child named Rain learned the names of every person in her town and made a game of greeting each one by name and the game became a habit and the habit became a reputation and the reputation became a relationship with an entire town that said: here is a person who sees you, who knows your name, who treats your existence as worth remembering. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woman who finds she loves people most clearly while watching them do the one thing they are truly best at.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman found that she loved people most clearly when they were doing the specific thing they were best at — a friend arranging flowers, her son absorbed in mathematics, a stranger playing piano in a station — and she collected these moments of people being most themselves as others collected paintings, and her collection was invisible and portable and the finest she knew. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy afraid of other people, who realizes other people are equally afraid of him, and what that mutual fear teaches him about beginning any relationship.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Arc was afraid of other people until he understood that other people were also afraid of him and that the fear was mutual and the mutuality made it manageable: two frightened people approaching each other carefully was the beginning of every relationship he had ever valued and the beginning was worth the fear. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who spends a year writing down one true thing about every person she meets, and what the finished notebook reveals about her own assumptions.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl spent a year writing down one true thing about every person she encountered and found at the year's end that she had a notebook full of true things about people that contradicted the easy categories she had put them in before she had started paying attention, and the notebook made her a better judge of character and a less reliable gossip and more interesting at dinner parties. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a man who remembers exactly what people told him years before and brings it back at exactly the right moment, becoming the person everyone calls to think things through.
tale: Once upon a time, a man named Cole was the kind of person who remembered what you had said three years ago and brought it back when it was relevant, not to score points but because he had been listening and he stayed listening and the listening never stopped. He was the person everyone called when they needed to think something through. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woman who inherits her mother's habit of feeding people and her father's habit of listening, and what her house becomes because of it.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman named Brea inherited her mother's habit of feeding people and her father's habit of listening, and the combination made her house the one everyone came to when they needed both, and she fed and listened for fifty years without calling it anything because it was simply what she did and the not calling it anything was part of what made it possible to sustain. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a child who realizes she has been the same person at four as she is at forty, and what she learns about the difference between changing and being clarified.
tale: Once upon a time, a child discovered that she had been the same person at four that she was at forty — the same curiosity, the same stubbornness, the same specific laughter — and that the years between had not changed her but had clarified her, stripped away the versions she had tried to be, left what was always there. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a man who spends his life surrounded by people and ends it peacefully alone, finding the solitude fitting rather than sad.
tale: Once upon a time, a man spent his life surrounded by people and ended it peacefully alone and found the alone not sad but fitting, because a person who has been fully among others and has given what they had to give arrives at solitude not as a failure but as a natural end, the way a conversation arrives at silence when everything worth saying has been said. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl told she is too sensitive, who eventually understands her sensitivity as a higher resolution of attention rather than a flaw.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Wren was told she was too sensitive and spent years trying to be less so before understanding that sensitivity was simply attention at a higher resolution — she noticed more, felt more, understood more — and the cost was proportion to the gain, and she stopped trying to reduce the resolution and started managing the cost. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who grows up watching his grandfather, the person the whole family turns to in difficulty, and the quality he learns is simply availability rather than wisdom.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy grew up watching his grandfather, who was the person everyone in the family came to in difficulty, and he learned from the watching that the useful quality was not wisdom but availability: his grandfather was always there, always willing to listen, never in a hurry, and the availability, sustained over decades, was indistinguishable from wisdom. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woman who understands people best through what they choose not to say, and becomes the finest counsellor in her region because of it.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman named Pal found that she understood people better through what they chose not to say than through what they said, and she spent her life attending to the silences in conversations and became the finest counsellor in her region, which people attributed to insight and which she attributed to having learned to be comfortable in silence and to let the silence do what words could not. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who is always the new person — new student, new colleague, new neighbour — and the instinct she develops for finding the one person worth knowing first.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl who was always the new person — the new student, the new colleague, the new neighbour — developed an extraordinary skill for finding quickly the one person in any group worth knowing first, and the skill was not calculation but instinct, the instinct of someone who had had to make friends quickly many times and had learned which small signs meant trustworthy. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a man who remembers every birthday and organises every leaving party at work, quietly maintaining the social fabric of a place worth spending forty hours a week inside.
tale: Once upon a time, a man named Fen was the person in every workplace who remembered birthdays and brought cake and organised the leaving gifts, and people thought of him as kind and he was kind but he was also doing something strategic: he was maintaining the social fabric of the place so that the place would be worth spending forty hours a week inside. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a child who asks every adult why they became what they became, and grows up to write the true answers down as a biographer.
tale: Once upon a time, a child named Seb had the habit of asking adults why they had become whatever they had become, and the adults he asked found the question unexpected and answering it required them to find a true answer rather than the professional one, and the true answers were the most interesting thing the child had ever heard and he grew up to be a biographer. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woman who notices that everyone she admires most was once profoundly wrong about something important and changed their mind, and what she learns from that pattern.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman found that the people she admired most had all had one formative period of being profoundly wrong about something important and had changed their minds, and the changing of the mind was the admirable thing rather than the final position, because a person who can be substantially wrong and substantially change is a person with more capacity for growth than a person who has always been right. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who discovers he is genuinely, reliably funny, and the year he finally lets someone close enough to see him when he isn't being funny at all.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Hal discovered that he was funny — genuinely, specifically, reliably funny — and spent years using the funniness as a way of keeping people at a comfortable distance until the year he decided to let someone close enough to see him not being funny, and the person he let close said: I like the funny but I like this better. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who grows up in a loud household where people argue and make up immediately, and what she learns about the difference between conflict and love.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl grew up in a household where the adults argued loudly and made up immediately and she learned that argument and love were not opposites and that the capacity to fight with someone was evidence of genuine relationship rather than its absence, and she spent her adult life bringing this understanding to people who had been taught that conflict meant something was wrong. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woman who is always first to arrive and last to leave every gathering, and what she discovers the in-between hours are actually for.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman named Jara was the person who arrived first and stayed last at every gathering, not out of loneliness but out of genuine investment in the time between arrival and leaving, which was where the real conversation happened, and she was known among her friends as the person who made parties worth having because she was the one who ensured the before and after were as rich as the middle. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a great oak at the centre of a village that has listened to four hundred years of conversation and gives advice by dropping its acorns with strange precision.
tale:
Once upon a time, a great oak at the centre of a village had been
listening to the village's conversations for four hundred years and
had developed, as a result, extremely good judgment.
It could not speak, but it dropped acorns with great precision —
one on the head of whoever was about to make a serious mistake, and
two at the feet of whoever needed encouragement.
The villagers learned to pay attention and after several generations
it became the most reliable source of quiet advice in the region.
When a young scholar came to study the phenomenon, the oak dropped
three acorns at once on the scholar's notebook, which everyone
agreed meant: you are asking the wrong questions.
And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who finds the oldest tree near her village, older than any record of the settlement itself, and learns to read centuries of weather from its fallen branches.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Rowan found a tree at the edge of her village that was older than any record of the village itself, and she spent her summers sitting beneath it reading the rings on fallen branches and reconstructing centuries of drought and flood and good years from the wood's own account. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a man who plants an oak acorn the day his daughter is born and tells her the tree is hers, and what they each become as they grow up together.
tale: Once upon a time, a man planted a single oak acorn on the day his daughter was born and told her the tree was hers. The tree grew as she grew and when she was married she held the ceremony beneath it, and when she was old it was older and still standing and she visited it as she would visit a sibling. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a forest that collectively decides one sacred grove deserves all its water and nutrients, and the unexplainable beauty that grows from that shared generosity.
tale: Once upon a time, a forest decided collectively that a particular grove at its centre was sacred and directed all its water and nutrients through the root network toward those trees, and those trees grew taller and stranger and more beautiful than any others in the region, and scientists who came to study them found evidence of the forest's generosity in the soil and wrote papers about it and still could not fully explain the choosing. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who climbs the tallest tree in his neighbourhood every morning before school, and what the quiet of the high branches teaches him.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy climbed the tallest tree in his neighbourhood every morning before school and sat in the highest branches watching the town wake up and felt, in the high quiet above the daily noise, that he could see things clearly enough to understand them. He became a philosopher. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woman who spends thirty years studying a single tree, not a forest, just one, and what she discovers about attention from never quite finishing.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman named Ash spent thirty years studying a single tree — not all trees, not forests, one tree — and found at the end of thirty years that she had only begun to understand it, and the not-ending of the understanding was the proof that attention given to one thing long enough revealed not the limits of the thing but the limits of attention. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a village that builds its meeting hall around the living roots of an enormous beech, and how meeting in the presence of something so old changes how people argue.
tale: Once upon a time, a village built its meeting hall around the root system of an enormous beech, incorporating the roots into the floor as benches and the trunk as the central column, and the village that conducted its arguments and celebrations in the presence of a living tree was notably calmer and more patient than the villages that met in rooms without trees. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who finds a hollow in an oak worn smooth into the shape of a chair by centuries of people sitting in it before her.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl found a hollow in an oak tree that was exactly the size and shape of a comfortable chair, worn smooth by centuries of people sitting in it before her, and she added herself to the centuries without knowing who the others had been and felt them there anyway. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woodworker who refuses to cut a living tree and works only with fallen wood, and the unusual biography each finished piece carries because of it.
tale: Once upon a time, a woodworker named Elm refused to cut a living tree and worked only with fallen wood, and the constraint made his furniture unusual because each piece came with a full biography — the dead elm found after the storm, the oak brought down by lightning — and the biography was part of what he gave his customers with the furniture. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a man who learns to read a forest the way a doctor reads a patient, understanding decades of history from the tilt and growth of its trees.
tale: Once upon a time, a man learned to read a forest the way a doctor reads a patient — the tilt of the trees told him about prevailing wind, the growth pattern told him about water, the moss told him about light — and he walked through forests knowing what had happened there for centuries before he arrived. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a child who plants a seed in a windowsill pot and waits months for any visible sign of life, and what the morning it finally sprouts teaches her about patience.
tale: Once upon a time, a child planted a seed in a pot on her windowsill and watched it for months without any visible result and then one morning found the first shoot pushing through the soil, and the patience required by the watching and the reward of the emergence taught her the specific lesson that nothing worth having arrives at the expected moment. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a town whose oldest tree has stood in the central square since its founding, its rings holding a complete unspoken history of every year that followed.
tale: Once upon a time, a town had a tree in its centre square that had been there since the town was founded and the tree's rings, if anyone had counted them, would have contained the whole history of the town, every drought the farmers had prayed against, every wet year the mills had profited from, every summer children had climbed its branches. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woman who builds her house in the branches of a tall pine and spends twenty years learning what the world looks like from a height most people never reach.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman built her house in the branches of a large pine and spent twenty years in the canopy, learning what the world looked like from the height of an old tree's crown, and the knowledge changed her understanding of ground-level life in ways she could not fully articulate but that made her more patient with what she could not see from where she stood. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a forest that burns and, thirty years later, reveals to a visiting botanist the precise and patient order in which it chose to grow itself back.
tale: Once upon a time, a forest burned and thirty years later a botanist studying the recovery found that the trees that grew first in the burned ground were not the same species that had grown there before but a pioneer community that prepared the soil for what would come after, and the forest's self-knowledge of what it needed in what sequence was more precise than any human intervention would have achieved. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who learns from his father, while pruning apple trees every winter, that cutting back is itself a form of care.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy grew up helping his father prune apple trees every winter and learned from the pruning that cutting back was a form of care and that removing what was not producing was the condition for what could produce, and he applied the principle to his own projects with consistent success. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who finds trees to be her truest, most reliable company, and the career she eventually builds from a childhood spent simply sitting among them.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Birch found that trees were her best company — not as a metaphor but practically, spending her afternoons among them preferring their silences to most people's conversations — and she grew up to study mycorrhizal networks and spent her career demonstrating that forests communicated more richly than anyone had previously understood, which was the scientific version of what she had known since childhood. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a man who spends a decade carving a single piece of fallen yew into a figure he says was already there, waiting only to be uncovered.
tale: Once upon a time, a man spent a decade carving a single piece of fallen yew into a figure he had seen in the grain of the wood, and the figure was already there, he said, and he had only removed what was not it, and when it was finished people who saw it felt it had been in the wood before the carving and simply needed the carver to find it. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a village elder who tells the children that the oldest tree in the forest has been listening to everyone who ever spoke beneath it, and how the children's honesty changes because of the story.
tale: Once upon a time, a village elder told the children that the oldest tree in the forest had been listening to people in the forest for six hundred years and remembered everything it had heard, and whether or not the children believed this literally, they became more honest in their conversations under the old tree than in any other location, and honesty practised in the company of the old tree became honesty practised everywhere. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who discovers she sleeps more deeply under trees than anywhere else, and the summer she spends testing which species gives the best rest.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl found that she slept better under trees than anywhere else, and she spent a summer sleeping outdoors beneath different species and found that oak gave her deep dreamless sleep and beech gave her vivid dreams and willow gave her something between sleep and waking that felt like listening, and she could not explain any of this but it was consistently true. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a town that cuts down its oldest tree to build a useful road, and the way the tree's name lingers in conversation for twenty years afterward.
tale: Once upon a time, a town cut down its oldest tree to build a road and the road was useful and the tree was gone and twenty years later the people of the town still mentioned the tree by name when they passed the road, the way you mention people who are gone, and the mentioning was the evidence that some things were worth more than their removal cost. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a man named Forest who spends his life replanting what has been lost, and lives just long enough to see his first plantings become true forests in their own right.
tale: Once upon a time, a man named Forest spent his life replanting forests where forests had been lost and lived to see his first planted trees become tall enough to house owls and his second plantings become forests in their own right, and the living to see it was the specific form of hope available only to people who work at the pace of trees. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who discovers every tree in her grandmother's garden was planted by her grandmother as a sapling, each one the shape of a future she imagined long before it existed.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl discovered that the trees in her grandmother's garden had all been planted by her grandmother as saplings, and the tall canopy that had shaded her entire childhood was her grandmother's imagination of the future made real and growing, and her grandmother had not known the trees as the shade they had become but had planted what they might become. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who learns the name of every tree in his neighbourhood and visits each one through the seasons, treating them as a community he is quietly responsible for.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy made friends with every tree in his neighbourhood by learning their names and visiting them seasonally and noting what was different, and the friendships were not with individuals in the human sense but were with specific living things in specific places that changed over time and could be known, and being known to him was his contribution and their being was theirs. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woman recovering from illness who spends her days under an old apple tree, finding comfort in its indifferent, reliable continuation through every season.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman spent her illness sitting in her garden under an old apple tree and found that the tree's company — its indifference to her illness, its reliable leafing and flowering and fruiting regardless of her health — was the most comforting fact of that period, proof that the world's ordinary processes were proceeding and would continue to proceed and that her illness was a feature of her particular life rather than of the world. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a man who finds a tree that grew slowly around an old fence, absorbing the wire and posts into itself, and what the sight teaches him about patience and obstacles.
tale: Once upon a time, a man found a tree that had grown around an old fence, incorporating the wire and the posts into itself over decades until the fence was inside the tree, and he found this the finest argument for patience he had ever encountered: given enough time, the living thing would incorporate even the obstruction. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a family who has planted a tree for every death across five generations, turning their garden into the most honest record of the family's grief and love.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl grew up in a family that planted a tree for every death and had been doing this for five generations, and the garden was therefore a dense and beautiful memorial, and she could walk through it and find the tree for each named ancestor, and the garden was the family's most honest account of itself, encoding grief as growth. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a fenced-off, logged wood that is left alone for twenty years and returns so completely to its original state that ecologists feel they are watching time reverse.
tale: Once upon a time, a wood was declared a nature reserve and fenced off from logging and within twenty years had returned to something so close to its original state that ecologists who studied old records could match the returning species to historical descriptions and felt they were watching time reverse, and the reversal was possible only because the trees had never stopped trying. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who finds an old tree whose roots have slowly lifted and cracked a city pavement, and the unexpected use she finds for the pattern years later.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl found an old tree whose roots had lifted the pavement of a city street, cracking the cement in a pattern that followed the root system exactly, and she photographed the crack pattern and later in her career as an architect used the root pattern as the basis for a city plan, reasoning that if roots found the most efficient path through resistant material they could guide the most efficient path through resistant urban space. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a man who inherits a neglected orchard and spends ten years learning the names and needs of its forgotten old fruit varieties to bring it back to life.
tale: Once upon a time, a man inherited a neglected orchard and spent ten years bringing it back, learning to identify the old varieties by their fruit and their leaf, restoring trees that had been unproductive for decades, and found in the restoration that the trees had simply been waiting for someone to know what they were and what they needed, and once known and given what they needed they gave everything back. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who spends her childhood inside a weeping willow beside a stream, learning from its branches the quiet, constant negotiation between trees and water.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Willow spent her childhood in a weeping willow tree that had grown beside a stream and whose branches reached the water, and she learned that trees and water were in constant negotiation — the tree offering shade and the water offering nourishment — and the negotiation was the ecological relationship and also the only relationship model she needed. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who finds that flowers bloom wherever she sets down her worries, and what people learn from naming what troubles them out loud.
tale:
Once upon a time, a girl named Ness found that flowers bloomed
wherever she placed her worries — she would set a worry down in the
garden like a stone, and by morning a flower had grown through it.
The flowers were unusual colours that had no names, and they lasted
exactly as long as the worry would have lasted had she kept it.
People came from far away to walk through her garden and found, to
their surprise, that naming what they were worried about and setting
it down made it smaller than it had seemed inside their chests.
Ness never ran out of worries, so the garden never stopped growing,
and in time it became the most visited place in the kingdom.
And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who discovers her grandmother's garden blooms in a sequence that tells a story across the year, if you know how to read it.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Iris discovered that the flowers in her grandmother's garden bloomed in a sequence that told a story if you watched it across a full year, beginning with snowdrops in the cold and ending with the last chrysanthemum before frost, and she learned to read the garden as a calendar written in colour. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woman who plants a single rose at the start of every difficult year of her life, building a garden that is a history of hardship survived.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman planted a single rose at the start of every difficult year of her life, and by the time she was old the garden contained a complete history of hardship survived, each rose blooming reliably despite or perhaps because of the year it commemorated. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who secretly waters a single wildflower growing through a crack in the pavement outside his apartment.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy found a single wildflower growing through a crack in the pavement outside his apartment and watered it secretly every evening, and the flower bloomed in defiance of the concrete around it, and he understood something about persistence that no lesson at school had managed to teach him. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl named Marigold who helps her mother dry flowers for winter, and what the kitchen smells like in the coldest months because of it.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Marigold spent her summers helping her mother dry flowers for winter, hanging bundles of lavender and chamomile from the kitchen rafters, and the kitchen in winter smelled of summer captured and held, and she understood preservation as a form of hope. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a man who has never grown anything successfully, who plants a simple window box and finds it changes the entire course of his life.
tale: Once upon a time, a man who had never grown anything successfully planted a window box of nasturtiums because they were said to be impossible to fail at, and they bloomed so abundantly that he became a gardener despite himself, working outward from that single easy success into roses and tomatoes and finally an entire allotment. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woman who lets part of her garden grow wild for ten years and discovers, on close inspection, how much had quietly arrived on its own.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman discovered that the wildflower meadow she had let grow wild for ten years contained, on close inspection, forty-three different species she had never planted, arrived by wind and bird and chance, and she understood that letting something be was sometimes the most productive gardening available. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who presses every flower she has ever been given by someone who loved her, building a book that becomes a record of being loved.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl made a pressed flower book of every flower she had been given by someone who loved her, and the book grew thicker through her life and was, by the time she was old, a complete record of being loved, pressed flat and kept safe between pages. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a man who spends forty years breeding a single dahlia toward a colour that doesn't yet exist, and what he does with his discovery once he's too old to enjoy it long.
tale: Once upon a time, a man spent forty years breeding a single variety of dahlia toward a colour that did not yet exist, and on the year he finally achieved it he was too old to enjoy many more seasons of it but gave the seeds to a younger gardener and felt the achievement was not diminished by his own limited time to enjoy it. And they all lived happily ever after.
