tale: Once upon a time, an amateur stargazer who lived in a city with terrible light pollution drove three hours every new moon to a dark patch of countryside purely to see the full sky properly, and said the drive itself had become, over the years, as much a part of the ritual as the stars at the end of it. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who collects the names of constellations from different cultures and discovers the same stars have been a bear, a plough, and a river to different people.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl who collected the names of constellations from every culture she could find discovered that the same group of stars was a bear in one tradition, a plough in another, and a great river crossing in a third, and concluded that the sky had always belonged equally to everyone who ever looked up at it. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a sailor's daughter who finally sees the Southern Cross herself after hearing her father describe it her whole life.
tale: Once upon a time, a sailor's daughter who had grown up hearing him describe the Southern Cross finally saw it herself on her first voyage south of the equator, and said the moment felt less like discovering something new and more like finally meeting someone she had heard about her entire life. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who excitedly points his first telescope at what he thinks is a bright new star, only to learn it is the planet Venus.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy who was learning to use his first telescope pointed it excitedly at what he was certain was an unusually bright new star, only for his older sister to explain, not unkindly, that it was in fact the planet Venus, and he kept the small disappointment to himself for exactly one evening before deciding Venus deserved his enthusiasm just as much. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about an elderly couple who have stargazed together every clear night for sixty years and can no longer remember which of them suggested it first.
tale: Once upon a time, an elderly couple who had been stargazing together every clear night for sixty years admitted, when finally asked, that they could no longer remember which of them had suggested it first, only that neither had ever wanted to stop. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a marine biologist whose research boat leaves a glowing trail of bioluminescent plankton that her crew has taken to calling a wake of fallen stars.
tale: Once upon a time, a marine biologist studying bioluminescent plankton said the glowing trail her research boat left at night looked so much like a wake of fallen stars that her crew had taken, without any official decision, to simply calling it that. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a child from a remote island with no artificial light, who realises on her first trip to a city that most people have never actually seen the full night sky.
tale: Once upon a time, a child growing up on a small remote island with almost no artificial light at night said she had not understood, until visiting a city for the first time, that most people in the world had never actually seen the full sky their entire lives. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a man recovering from illness who asks to be wheeled outside one clear night just to look at the stars and feel his troubles made appropriately small.
tale: Once upon a time, a man recovering from a long illness asked to have his hospital bed wheeled briefly outside one clear night specifically to look at the stars, saying that something about their enormous, patient indifference made his own difficulties feel, for just a few minutes, appropriately small. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about an astronomer who spends her career studying one distant star system and finally finds evidence it may host a planet of its own.
tale: Once upon a time, an astronomer who had spent her entire career studying a single distant star system finally received data suggesting it might host a planet of its own, and said the years of patient, mostly uneventful observation suddenly felt, in that one afternoon, entirely worth it. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl whose father in the merchant navy always draws a small star on his postcards marking the constellation overhead the night he wrote them.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl who grew up with a father in the merchant navy said the postcards he sent always included, in the corner, a small hand-drawn star marking whichever constellation was directly overhead the night he wrote it, so she always knew exactly what sky he was standing under even when she didn't know exactly where. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about two strangers who lie side by side on an airport observation deck during a delayed overnight flight, identifying constellations together.
tale: Once upon a time, two strangers waiting out a delayed overnight flight ended up lying side by side on the cold floor of an airport observation deck, having discovered they shared an interest in identifying constellations through the wide windows, and neither one ever quite forgot the conversation that followed. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a retired teacher who spent forty years teaching the night sky to disinterested teenagers, and the former students who come back years later just to say it stayed with them.
tale: Once upon a time, a retired teacher who had spent forty years explaining the night sky to disinterested teenagers said the single best part of the job, every single year without exception, was the one or two students who came back years later just to say that the lesson on stars was the only thing from school they still actually thought about. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about an old car that refuses to start on the most important morning of its owner's life, and what the delay unexpectedly saves him from.
tale: Once upon a time, a man named Arlo had a battered green car that had never once let him down, until the morning of the biggest interview of his life, when it simply refused to turn over. He pushed and cursed and called a mechanic, arriving two hours late to find the company had been raided by inspectors that very morning and the interview room turned into an evidence room. The car had known something Arlo had not, and it never let him down again. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a small town mechanic who can hear what is wrong with any car before she opens the bonnet.
tale: Once upon a time, a mechanic named Paz could diagnose any car by sound alone — a particular knock told her about a crankshaft, a certain wheeze about a head gasket — and people drove hundreds of kilometres just to have her listen to their engines for thirty seconds. She never charged for the listening, only the fixing, and she said the two were entirely different skills. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a car that has been parked in the same spot for forty years and the neighbourhood that has grown up around it.
tale: Once upon a time, a cream-coloured car was parked outside a bakery for what its owner intended to be one hour and became, through a series of forgotten errands and a sudden move abroad, forty years. The street grew around it: children learned to ride bicycles around it, a couple had their first kiss leaning against it, and the baker started putting a small pot of flowers on its roof each spring. When the owner's grandchildren finally came to collect it, the whole street turned out to say goodbye. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who inherits her grandmother's car and discovers it still smells like her grandmother's particular perfume regardless of how many times it is cleaned.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Dara inherited her late grandmother's old saloon and found that no matter how many times she vacuumed the seats or sprinkled bicarbonate, the car always smelled faintly of rose water and something else she could not name. She stopped trying to remove the smell on the day she sat in it crying after a difficult week and found the scent was, in fact, exactly what she needed, like being held from a great distance. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about two strangers who discover their cars are identical in every detail, including the same dent in the same door.
tale: Once upon a time, two strangers named Bram and Sela parked side by side in a supermarket car park and noticed their cars were not merely the same model but the same faded blue, the same cracked wing mirror, the same dent on the rear left door in the exact same place. They stood looking from one car to the other for a long time and then, having nothing else to do, went for coffee and talked for three hours about all the other things in their lives that had quietly become identical without their noticing. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a long-distance road trip that teaches a father and daughter something they could not have learned at home.
tale: Once upon a time, a father and daughter who had run out of things to say to each other agreed to drive across the country together because neither could think of a reason not to. By the third day the silences between them had changed quality entirely, becoming the kind that did not need filling, and somewhere in the middle of a wide flat plain the daughter told him about the thing she had been afraid to tell him for two years and he took his hand off the wheel briefly to put it on her shoulder. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a car that runs perfectly for everyone except its registered owner.
tale: Once upon a time, a man named Cob owned a car that started every single time for his wife, his children, his neighbours, and a complete stranger who borrowed it once in an emergency, but stalled without fail every time Cob himself got behind the wheel. Mechanics could find nothing wrong. His wife said the car simply had a preference and he should stop taking it personally, which he found difficult until he accepted it and started asking his wife to drive, and found he quite liked being a passenger. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a vintage car restorer who falls in love with a car she has been commissioned to sell.
tale: Once upon a time, a restorer named Vee spent four months bringing a 1962 roadster back to life — sourcing the original paint colour, re-stitching the leather, coaxing the engine back to something better than new — and on the day she was supposed to hand it to its new owner she sat in it for one last minute and understood she had made an irreversible mistake in pricing. She bought it back from the buyer three months later at considerable personal expense and drove it every weekend for thirty years. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a town where everyone's car reflects its owner's personality without anyone having chosen it that way.
tale: Once upon a time, in a small valley town, people noticed that their cars had begun to match them in ways they could not explain: the postmistress drove an immaculate white hatchback that never got dirty, the carpenter drove a battered workhorse that always had tools in the boot he had not put there, and the town's most anxious resident drove a car that needed jump-starting every single morning regardless of the battery's actual charge. Nobody could say when it had started, only that it was entirely accurate. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who grows up beside a motorway and comes to know the sound of every type of passing vehicle by heart.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Fen grew up in a house that backed onto a motorway and learned to identify every type of vehicle by sound without looking: the low drone of a fully loaded lorry, the specific pitch of a sports car under acceleration, the quiet swish of an electric car that you heard only after it had already passed. When he grew up he became an acoustic engineer and said the motorway had been a better education than any classroom he had sat in. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a car whose radio only ever plays the song a passenger most needs to hear at that exact moment.
tale: Once upon a time, a taxi driver named Ro noticed that her car radio always seemed to find the right song for whoever was in the back seat: a song from a passenger's wedding year when they were heading to a divorce lawyer, a song a grieving man had last heard with his mother, a ridiculous pop hit that made a crying child laugh. She stopped trying to control it and let the radio do what it clearly knew better, and she kept driving that same car until it simply would not move anymore. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woman who buys her first car at the age of seventy and the freedom it brings her.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman named Hel bought her first car at the age of seventy after a lifetime of relying on buses, family, and good timing, and spent her first free Saturday simply driving somewhere with no particular reason to be there. She arrived at a coastal village she had meant to visit for fifty years and had a plate of fish and chips looking out at the sea, alone and entirely content, and drove home humming. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about two siblings who disagree about what to do with their late father's car.
tale: Once upon a time, a brother and sister inherited their father's ancient estate car and could not agree: the brother wanted to sell it and divide the money, the sister wanted to keep it because she could still smell their father's jacket in the upholstery. They drove it together to the coast where they had spent childhood holidays and parked at the top of the cliff where their father had always stopped, and sat for an hour looking at the sea before the brother quietly suggested they might both simply need more time. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a car that always finds a parking space, even in the most impossible city.
tale: Once upon a time, a city dweller named Sol drove a small yellow car that had the inexplicable quality of always finding a space precisely where it was needed: outside the hospital on the worst day, directly in front of the florist when running late for an anniversary, at the exact entrance to the theatre on the night the street was completely otherwise full. Sol never mentioned this to anyone because he thought they would not believe him, and also because he found it more peaceful to keep at least one reliable miracle to himself. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a driving instructor whose students always go on to do something remarkable with the independence their licence gives them.
tale: Once upon a time, a driving instructor named Marta taught in the same small town for thirty years and kept a quiet list of what her former students did with their new independence: one drove to the coast and never came back inland, one used her licence to take a job that required weekly travel and became a regional manager, one simply drove himself to the hospital for tests he had been putting off for years. Marta said a driving licence was not really about driving. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a car that is passed down through five generations of the same family and what each generation adds to it.
tale: Once upon a time, a black saloon was bought new by a great-great-grandmother named Ida and passed to each subsequent generation with one rule: you could repair it but not replace it, and whatever small object you needed from the glovebox could stay there. By the fifth generation the glovebox contained a cassette tape, a hospital parking receipt, a child's drawing, a love letter, and a single earring, and the car had been repainted twice but still had Ida's original door handles, which nobody had been able to bring themselves to change. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a man who lives in his car by choice, not necessity, and the people he meets because of it.
tale: Once upon a time, a retired architect named Ors sold his house and took up residence in his large estate car, not because he had to but because he had calculated that a house required fourteen hours a week of maintenance and he wanted those hours back. He parked in laybys and car parks and outside friends' houses and discovered that people knocked on his window at all hours to ask if he was all right, and the knocking led to conversations, and the conversations were the best thing that had happened to him since his wife had died. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who learns more about her grandfather from the contents of his car's boot than from anything he ever told her directly.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Bex was given the task of clearing out her late grandfather's car and found in the boot: a folded ordnance survey map with a single route marked in pencil, a pair of women's walking boots three sizes smaller than her grandmother's, a jar of jam with no label, and a photograph of a mountain she did not recognise. She spent the following summer following the pencil route and found, at the end of it, a small farm where an elderly woman had known her grandfather for twenty years under a different name. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a car that was used as a getaway vehicle in a minor crime forty years ago and the embarrassing secret its current owner discovers.
tale: Once upon a time, a respectable retired librarian named Dot bought a secondhand car and later discovered, through a local history article, that her precise make, model, colour, and registration had been used to steal fourteen garden gnomes from a park in 1983 as a student prank and had never been caught. She framed the article and put it in her study and found it made her considerably more interesting at dinner parties than forty years of library work had managed. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a car that breaks down at the exact right place at the exact right time.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman named Ren was driving to end a relationship she had been unhappy in for years when her car stopped dead outside a pottery studio she had never noticed before. While waiting for the mechanic she went inside and spent two hours learning to centre clay on a wheel, which she discovered she was unexpectedly good at, and signed up for lessons the following week, and the week after, and the month after, and by the time the relationship ended naturally six months later she had something entirely her own already waiting. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who discovers her late mother's old bicycle still knows the route to every place her mother loved.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Calla inherited her mother's rusty bicycle and found, on her first ride, that it turned left at the end of the lane without any pressure from her hands, leading her to a bakery she had never visited, a bench by a river she had never found, and finally to a small garden behind a church where her mother had apparently spent every Friday morning for twenty years. The bicycle stopped there and would not move until Calla had sat in the garden for a while, and she went back every Friday after that. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a bicycle repair man who can tell from the damage on a bicycle exactly what kind of life its owner lives.
tale: Once upon a time, a repair man named Wick could read a bicycle the way others read a face: worn brake pads on the right told him the rider lived at the top of a hill, a chain stretched unevenly told him the rider was always in a hurry on the left side of the street, and a saddle worn smooth in the front told him the rider was always leaning forward into something. He never shared his readings unless asked, and when asked, he was always right. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who learns to ride a bicycle at the age of forty and what the achievement unlocks in him.
tale: Once upon a time, a man named Tor had failed to learn to ride a bicycle as a child and carried the small humiliation quietly for thirty years until his daughter, aged seven, simply handed him her bicycle at the bottom of a gentle slope and told him to try. He fell twice, wobbled catastrophically, and on the third attempt rode to the end of the street. He bought his own bicycle the following weekend and cycled to work every day for the next fifteen years, feeling, each morning, like someone who had just learned something important. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a city that outlaws all vehicles except bicycles for one month and what the city discovers about itself.
tale: Once upon a time, a city council passed a one-month bicycle-only law as an experiment and expected chaos. Instead the city became quieter, people started nodding at each other on the street, the air tasted different, and three neighbourhoods that had been separated by fast roads for decades began, tentatively, to mix. When the month ended and the cars returned, a third of the city's residents simply kept cycling and the council never quite explained why the experiment had not been extended. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woman who cycles across a country to deliver a letter she could have sent by post.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman named Ola received a letter she needed to reply to in a way that no postal service could carry and borrowed a bicycle and rode it across the whole country to deliver the reply by hand. The journey took three weeks. When she arrived the person she had written to was sitting on the front step as though expecting her, and said simply: I thought it might be something like that. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a bicycle that is stolen and found a decade later in another city, having apparently been ridden across the country.
tale: Once upon a time, a man named Bram had his bicycle stolen from outside his office and found it, ten years later, leaning outside a café in a city four hundred kilometres away, still with his name scratched under the saddle. The bicycle was in better condition than when he had owned it, the tyres recently replaced and the chain newly oiled, and the café owner said it had simply appeared one morning and no one had ever claimed it. Bram left it where it was. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about two rival bicycle shops on the same street and the unexpected way their competition ends.
tale: Once upon a time, two bicycle shops faced each other across a narrow street and competed so fiercely for forty years that the owners, Nan and Pete, had not spoken a civil word since 1987. When both shops were damaged in the same storm on the same night, their respective children, who had grown up as reluctant friends, repaired both shops together while the parents were in hospital, and somewhere in the shared work the feud simply ran out of material. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who wins a bicycle race she was not supposed to enter.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Sev entered a bicycle race by mistake — she had meant to sign up for a baking competition in the next tent — and won it despite having trained for neither event. The trophy was too large to carry on a bicycle and she had to push it home in a wheelbarrow borrowed from a garden centre, which she considered a reasonable price for an accidental victory. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a grandfather who builds his granddaughter's first bicycle entirely from spare parts and the story each part carries.
tale: Once upon a time, a grandfather named Eli built his granddaughter's first bicycle from parts he had gathered across his life: the frame from a market, the wheels from a delivery bicycle he had ridden as a young man, the bell from a bicycle his own father had once owned. When he gave it to her he told her each part's story, and she rode into the street carrying, without knowing it, three generations of the family's particular relationship with getting somewhere under their own power. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a bicycle messenger who is the only person in the city who knows where everything is.
tale: Once upon a time, a bicycle messenger named Rue had cycled every street of the city so many times that she kept its entire layout in her body rather than her head — she turned without thinking, found detours without deciding, and arrived before expected without understanding how. People who were lost stopped asking their phones and started waiting to spot Rue instead, knowing she would be along within the quarter-hour and that she always knew. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who cycles to school in all weathers for five years and what the habit teaches him.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Arc cycled to school every single day for five years regardless of weather, arriving wet in autumn, frozen in winter, and covered in pollen in spring. His teachers noted that he was the only student who consistently noticed what the sky was doing, what the street smelled like, and what his body felt like at different times of year. He later said the cycling had given him a relationship with the actual world that sitting in cars had prevented in his classmates. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woman who teaches her entire village to ride bicycles over one summer.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman named Flo arrived in a mountain village where no one had ever learned to cycle and spent a summer teaching every resident from seven to seventy-three. She used the same method for all ages: she removed the pedals until balance was learned, then added them back, and she was patient with everyone equally. By autumn the village had a cycling club and had named a trail after her, and she moved on to the next village carrying only a bag of tools and more patience than anyone had expected. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a bicycle that can only be ridden by someone who is telling the truth.
tale: Once upon a time, there was a bicycle in a village square that anyone could try but that simply would not move unless its rider was being honest — not necessarily out loud, but honest in the deep interior way that mattered. Most adults could not make it move at all. Children rode it easily. One old man got it to move a full three metres before it stopped, which was considered the best adult performance in thirty years. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a long-distance cyclist who discovers that the journey changes depending on the direction she travels.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman named Cass cycled the same route from east to west, rested, and cycled it back from west to east, and found that the two journeys were so different in their demands, their light, their smells, and the conversations they produced that she was not sure they were the same road at all. She spent twenty years cycling the same routes in both directions and wrote a book about it that cyclists said described their own experience exactly, though they had never found the words before. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a child's bicycle left outside a hospital that becomes the start of something unexpectedly larger.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl left her bicycle unlocked outside a hospital while visiting her grandmother and found it gone when she came out. A nurse who had witnessed the confusion began a hospital lending scheme with donated bicycles for patients' families who needed to travel home after long visits, and within a year the scheme had thirty bicycles and a small shed and a waiting list. The girl's bicycle, repainted, was the one used most often. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a man who fixes other people's bicycles for free outside his house on Saturday mornings.
tale: Once upon a time, a retired engineer named Dom set a folding table and his tools outside his house every Saturday morning and fixed whatever broken bicycles people brought him, charging nothing. In the first month three people came. By the end of the year there was a small queue by nine o'clock, and people brought coffee and bread and sometimes sat for a while even after their bicycle was done, which Dom said was the part he had not planned for and the part he liked most. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who rides her bicycle into a part of the forest where she is quite sure no path existed before.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Bea followed a path into the forest that she was quite certain had not been there last week and found, at its end, a clearing where someone had recently been: a circle of flattened grass, a small pile of stones, and a single bicycle bell left on a stump in perfect condition. She took the bell, added it to her own bicycle, and the path was never there again, though her bell rang sometimes when there was no wind. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a town that remembers every bicycle that has ever passed through it.
tale: Once upon a time, the oldest inhabitants of a small town could describe, if you asked, every bicycle that had passed through in their lifetimes: the blue racer from the 1960s with the white ribbon, the loaded touring bike from the German cyclist who stayed three days, the child's tricycle that had belonged to someone who later came back as an adult on a different bicycle entirely. The town did not write these things down because the remembering was the point. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woman who discovers that cycling the same route every day for a year makes her invisible to ordinary worry.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman named Ren began cycling the same three-kilometre loop around the park every morning as a way of managing a period of great anxiety and found, after six months, that the anxiety had not gone but had become smaller relative to the loop, which was always the same and always completed and always sufficient. By the end of the year she could not easily explain to anyone else how this had worked, but she recommended it to everyone who asked. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about two children who share a single bicycle between them and the system they devise.
tale: Once upon a time, two siblings named Mira and Seb had only one bicycle between them and devised a system: whoever needed it more urgently that day had priority, and urgency was determined by a short negotiation that rarely took more than two minutes and in which both parties were surprisingly honest. Their parents, watching from the window, said the bicycle had taught the children more about fairness and compromise than any lesson they had been given directly, and they never bought a second one. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a dragon who is afraid of fire and the unlikely friendship she builds because of it.
tale: Once upon a time, a young dragon named Sela could not produce a single flame without flinching, which made her the subject of considerable teasing among her kin and drove her into the company of a blacksmith's apprentice who was equally afraid of his own forge. The two spent their days together on a cold hillside, the dragon learning courage from the boy's gradual mastery of his fear, and the boy learning that everyone had something that frightened them and that this was not a personal failing. By spring both of them had found their fire, and they remained friends long after both had stopped needing to be afraid. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a very old dragon who has forgotten she is a dragon and lives quietly among humans.
tale: Once upon a time, a very old dragon named Dara had lived so long in human form that she had entirely forgotten she was anything else, and spent her days running a small bakery and complaining about the damp. It was only when a young child pointed at her shadow one afternoon, which was unmistakably wing-shaped, that she began to remember, piece by piece, across several weeks of quiet recollection. She kept the bakery. Some things, she decided, suited her better than mountains ever had. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a kingdom that solves all its disputes by asking its resident dragon for a second opinion.
tale: Once upon a time, a kingdom discovered that its dragon, Corm, had extraordinarily good judgment and an entirely neutral perspective on human affairs, having no stake in any of them. The courts began sending difficult cases to the mountain, and Corm received them with mild irritation and complete accuracy, pointing out what everyone already knew but could not say for reasons of politics or embarrassment. The kingdom became unusually well-governed for two hundred years and attributed this, in official documents, to wisdom and leadership, though the dragon knew better. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who discovers a dragon egg in her vegetable garden and raises the hatchling in secret.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Bex dug up a smooth, warm stone in the garden that was very clearly not a stone and hid it in her bedroom wardrobe under three jumpers. Three weeks later it hatched into something the size of a cat that immediately set fire to the jumpers. She named it Pip, taught it to eat only from a fireproof bowl, and kept it hidden for two years until Pip was the size of a wardrobe itself and the hiding was no longer practical. Her parents were considerably more understanding than she had expected. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a dragon who collects not gold but something entirely unexpected.
tale: Once upon a time, a dragon named Fen was famous throughout the mountain ranges not for her hoard of gold — she had very little interest in gold — but for her collection of apologies, which she accepted from anyone who offered a genuine one and stored in a vast cavern where they glowed faintly according to their sincerity. Travellers came from distant lands to see them, and Fen found that the apologies, over centuries, had given her a more complete understanding of human nature than any amount of treasure could have. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about the last dragon in the world and the librarian who keeps her existence secret.
tale: Once upon a time, the last dragon in the world lived in the basement of a very large library and was known only to the head librarian, a woman named Sol who had inherited the secret from the previous head librarian along with the keys and the pension. The dragon had spent three hundred years reading everything in the building and was an excellent research assistant, provided you asked quietly and did not mind the occasional singed edge. Sol saw no reason to tell anyone. Some things were better kept between a librarian and a dragon. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a dragon who is a very poor flyer and the humiliating career change this forces upon her.
tale: Once upon a time, a dragon named Roo had the misfortune of being constitutionally unable to fly in a straight line, veering left regardless of intention and landing in places she had not planned to be, usually someone's roof. After considerable property damage and several sincere apologies, she retrained as a cartographer and found that her tendency to go where she had not intended had given her an unusually thorough knowledge of the terrain. Her maps were the most accurate in the region and noted several features that no straight-flying dragon would ever have found. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a young knight who is sent to slay a dragon and comes back having done something else entirely.
tale: Once upon a time, a young knight named Tor was sent to slay the dragon of the eastern mountain and returned three months later with a full report on the dragon's habits, diet, and preferred conversational topics but without having slain anything. He said the dragon had been extremely reasonable and also significantly larger than his sword, and that he felt the kingdom's resources were better spent on diplomacy. The king, who had privately hoped for this outcome, promoted him. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a dragon who has been asleep for a thousand years and wakes to find the world entirely changed.
tale: Once upon a time, a dragon named Eld woke from a thousand-year sleep in a cave that now had a motorway running past its entrance and spent the first month simply watching the cars go by with intense interest before concluding they were a kind of very fast wingless dragon without fire, which was close enough to accurate that she felt no need to revise the assessment. She adapted to the new world with the pragmatism that great age tends to produce and eventually found herself working in a museum that had been built on the site of her old cave, where she was considerably more knowledgeable than any of her colleagues. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a dragon and a lighthouse keeper who become unlikely companions.
tale: Once upon a time, a dragon named Ash began landing on the lighthouse promontory each evening at dusk, attracted initially by the warmth of the light and then by the company of the keeper, a solitary man named Gray who said very little but made excellent tea. They spent years in companionable near-silence, Ash watching the sea and Gray watching the light, and when ships saw two sources of flame on the headland — one mechanical, one biological — they steered safely clear of both. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a village that has never seen a dragon before and the very different stories each resident tells about what arrived.
tale: Once upon a time, a small green dragon landed in the village square of a place that had never seen a dragon, stayed for approximately ten minutes to eat a dropped pie, and departed. The accounts given afterward by the seventeen residents who witnessed this disagreed on every point except the pie: some described a creature the size of a horse, others the size of a cart horse, one the size of a house. The colour ranged from dark green to purple. One woman was certain it had spoken to her. It had not spoken, but she had said something to it, and perhaps that was close enough. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a dragon who has lost her memory and must travel with a child to find out who she is.
tale: Once upon a time, a dragon with no memory of her own name was found sleeping in an orchard by a ten-year-old named Mira, who was small enough not to be frightened and curious enough to ask questions. The two travelled together across four kingdoms following the clues embedded in the dragon's body — a scar she did not remember getting, a marking on her wing that matched a symbol on a particular ancient gate — and found her origin, which was considerably more complicated than either had anticipated. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a dragon who teaches at a school and the annual problem of the science fair.
tale: Once upon a time, a dragon named Vel taught natural history at a small school and found the role entirely satisfying except for the annual science fair, in which students invariably asked her to demonstrate fire for their projects on combustion. She had a strict policy against it indoors, which she held to firmly, but she made an exception one year for a student who was studying bioluminescence and needed a very precise small flame in a specific colour, and the resulting project won the regional prize and Vel was given a commendation, which she found disproportionate. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who raises a dragon and must eventually let it go.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Eli raised a dragon from an egg the size of a melon to a creature too large to enter the house and then too large to enter the garden, and he fed it and talked to it and taught it what he knew, which was considerable for a twelve-year-old and modest by any other standard. On the morning the dragon first lifted from the ground without coming back down, Eli watched until she was a speck and then a memory, and understood something about love and sufficiency that most people did not learn until much later. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a dragon who refuses to breathe fire on principle.
tale: Once upon a time, a dragon named Paz had decided, after considerable thought, that breathing fire solved fewer problems than it created and declined on principle to do it. Other dragons found this eccentric and occasionally inconvenient, but Paz found that the absence of the option forced her toward solutions that were generally more interesting and more durable: negotiation, patience, strategic withdrawal, and on one memorable occasion, an extremely well-timed sneeze that achieved approximately the same result as fire would have but with considerably less collateral damage. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a dragon who guards not a treasure but a story, and who will only let you pass if you give her a better one.
tale: Once upon a time, a dragon named Ora guarded the pass through the Grey Mountains not for gold but for stories, which she collected in the same way others collected coins, weighing each one for truth and originality and emotional resonance. Travellers who brought her a genuine story — not a borrowed one, not an embellished one, but something true and specific that had actually happened — were allowed through immediately. Those who brought obvious fabrications were sent back without malice, only the observation that they might try again once something worth telling had occurred to them. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a dragon who is the best listener anyone has ever encountered.
tale: Once upon a time, a dragon named Cass became known across seven kingdoms not for her power or her hoard but for her quality of attention. People climbed the mountain simply to tell her things — their regrets, their confusions, their decisions they could not make alone — and she listened with the full weight of three hundred years and asked, occasionally, one question that was exactly the right one. She never gave advice unless asked, and even then sparingly, because she had found that most people did not actually need advice and simply needed to be heard by something very old and very patient. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a dragon who goes to considerable lengths to avoid being heroically defeated.
tale: Once upon a time, a dragon named Wren had survived four centuries by the simple strategy of leaving any situation that had started to resemble a fairy tale: the moment a young person with a sword appeared on the mountain trail she was already packing her smaller valuables, and she had relocated twelve times in two hundred years, always to somewhere less narratively convenient. She was widely considered to be the cleverest dragon alive, which she felt was a low bar given how the others kept getting themselves slain. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a dragon who is an exceptional cook and runs a popular restaurant.
tale: Once upon a time, a dragon named Flint discovered that precision flame control, applied to food rather than enemies, produced results that no conventional oven could match: a crust on a roast that was exactly three seconds from perfection, a brûlée that required a quarter-second burst at a specific temperature, a bread that could be proved and baked in the same afternoon. The restaurant had a six-month waiting list and Flint refused all requests for catering events, as she found crowds of humans more difficult to manage than any culinary challenge. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a dragon and a princess who become friends before anyone sends a knight to rescue the princess.
tale: Once upon a time, a princess named Ren arrived at a dragon's mountain, not having been carried there by anyone but having walked up herself out of curiosity, and found the dragon — a large, largely indifferent creature named Sol — willing enough to talk. They spent a week in animated conversation about geography, history, and the comparative stupidity of the quests people undertook without sufficient preparation, and when the knight finally arrived, breathless from the climb, both of them looked at him with the mild impatience of people who have been interrupted at something more interesting. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight who is terrible at fighting but extraordinarily good at finding things.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Fen was the worst swordsman in the kingdom and the finest finder of lost things in living memory. He could locate a missing ring in a forest, a stolen horse in a city of ten thousand, and, on one occasion, a king's misplaced treaty before the war it would have prevented began. The king eventually stopped sending him to battles and started sending him to situations, which was a different and considerably more useful thing. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woman who becomes a knight not through combat but through an act of extraordinary patience.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman named Ora was granted a knighthood after sitting beside a sick child for seventeen days without sleep until the fever broke, having been told by every physician that the child would not survive. The king, who had been told the same thing, said that what she had done required more courage than any battle he had witnessed, and he knighted her on the spot with the first implement to hand, which was a bread knife, and she wore the scar from the ceremony proudly for the rest of her life. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a very old knight who goes on one last quest, not for glory but to return something he took forty years ago.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Ash who had fought in twelve campaigns and won eleven of them packed a saddlebag at the age of seventy-two and rode four days to return a small bronze cup he had taken from a defeated enemy's camp in his first war, which he had thought of every morning since. The family still had the shelf where it belonged. They gave him dinner and a bed and he rode home the next day lighter than he had felt in forty years. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight whose armour reflects the truth about whoever looks at it.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Sol wore armour that had been forged by a smith with unusual ideas about honesty, and whatever a person saw when they looked at Sol's armour was a perfectly accurate reflection of what they most needed to confront about themselves. Enemies frequently stopped mid-charge. Courtiers avoided him at banquets. The king consulted him before every difficult decision, not for military advice but for the sake of standing near enough to the armour to see clearly for a few minutes. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight who becomes famous not for slaying monsters but for talking to them.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Paz discovered that the monsters she was sent to defeat were, in almost every case, considerably more interesting to talk to than to fight, and that most of them had grievances that were at least partially justified. She developed a reputation over twenty years as someone you sent when you wanted the problem resolved rather than merely concluded, and the kingdom's borders became considerably more stable than they had been when the previous approach had been exclusively swords. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a young knight's squire who is clearly more capable than the knight.
tale: Once upon a time, a squire named Bex was assigned to a knight named Lord Cob who was well-meaning, enthusiastic, and reliably wrong about almost everything tactical. Bex quietly managed every situation from three steps behind, redirected charges, re-read maps, and revised plans in real time, and when the king eventually summoned them both to ask which of the two had actually won the campaign, Lord Cob immediately and sincerely said it was Bex, which was the most accurate military assessment he had ever made. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight who is afraid of horses and the campaign this considerably complicates.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Tor had won every tournament he had ever entered on foot and had never once admitted he was terrified of horses, a fact that became increasingly difficult to conceal during a campaign that required crossing a river on horseback in full armour at speed. He crossed the river, white-knuckled and silent, and when they made camp on the other side his squire simply handed him a cup of tea without comment, and that was that, and they never discussed it. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a kingdom that sends its most diplomatic knight rather than its most powerful one to resolve a border dispute.
tale: Once upon a time, a kingdom with three powerful knights chose to send the quietest and least decorated of the three to negotiate a border dispute with a neighbouring nation, on the grounds that she listened more than she spoke and had never once started a conflict she had not already finished. She returned six days later with a treaty, two new trade agreements, and an invitation to a wedding, having apparently spent most of the time asking questions about the other kingdom's cheese, which she found genuinely interesting. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight who keeps a detailed journal of every quest and what the journal eventually becomes.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Ren kept a meticulous journal of every quest she undertook: not the heroic parts, but the weather, the cost of lodging, the quality of the roads, what she had eaten, what had actually worked and what had merely looked impressive. After forty years the journal was sixteen volumes and had become, without her intending it, the most accurate practical guide to the kingdom's geography, politics, and logistics that had ever been written, and the army used it as a field manual for the following century. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight who is sent on a quest to find something that turns out to already be where she started.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Mira spent three years crossing mountains and oceans searching for a source of pure water that a prophecy had described in elaborate and poetic terms, and returned to find that the spring in her own family's garden matched every detail precisely and always had. She sat beside it for a long time before going inside for dinner. She did not mention the three years to anyone, and she visited the spring every morning after that with a great deal more attention than she had previously given it. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a retired knight who teaches children and finds it the hardest thing she has ever done.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Dara who had survived thirty years of campaigning retired to teach swordsmanship to the children of the capital and found, within the first week, that it was significantly more demanding than any battlefield she had crossed. The children were unpredictable, enthusiastic, entirely without fear, and impossible to reason with before noon. She stayed for fifteen years and said in old age that teaching had required more courage, patience, and creativity than anything she had done in armour. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about two knights on opposite sides of a war who meet at the same inn each evening.
tale: Once upon a time, two knights from opposing armies found themselves lodging at the same inn on the border each evening during a slow campaign, having both discovered it was the only establishment within twenty miles with reliable hot water. They ate at separate tables for the first week, acknowledged each other in the second, and were sharing a meal by the third, at which point the war ended and they realised they had no particular reason to stop. They remained correspondents for forty years. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight whose greatest weapon is an extraordinarily accurate memory.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Cas won every negotiation, every ambush defence, and every political contest not through strength but through a memory so precise that she could recall every word spoken in any conversation she had ever had. She remembered promises made a decade ago, remembered who had said what in which room, and remembered, crucially, the small details that people revealed when they thought they were not being observed. Kings found her invaluable and slightly unnerving, which was exactly the right combination. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight who discovers mid-quest that the person who sent her on it was wrong about everything.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Sev was sent to retrieve a stolen crown from a thief in the northern provinces and arrived to find that the crown had been given freely, that the thief was actually the crown's rightful owner, and that the person who had sent her on the quest had known this and simply hoped the situation would resolve itself violently and from a distance. She returned the crown, wrote a very clear report, and resigned her commission, which she felt was the most knightly thing she had done in years. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight who is most formidable not in battle but in the hour after it.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Gray was considered by his peers to be merely adequate with a sword but exceptional in the aftermath of any conflict: he was the one who organised the wounded, negotiated the terms, ensured the supplies were distributed fairly, and wrote the letters to families that no one else could face writing. Soldiers who had fought beside him for years said privately that they had felt safer because of what he did after the battle than anything he had done during it. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight who falls asleep during her own victory banquet and what people discover about her because of it.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Lira fell asleep at her own victory banquet before the second course and was found by a page with her cheek on the tablecloth and a hand still loosely around a bread roll, and the assembled court, rather than being offended, was collectively moved to understand for the first time how tired she had actually been for the past two years, and several people quietly resolved to say thank you properly in the morning. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight sent to guard a library and what the assignment unexpectedly teaches her.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Ivy was assigned to guard a large royal library as a punishment for an administrative error and spent six months in increasingly reluctant proximity to the books before, out of sheer boredom, beginning to read them. She read military history, navigation, engineering, medicine, and finally poetry, and returned from her posting a significantly better knight than she had arrived, which the official who had assigned the post had not intended but did not regret. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight who is knighted by mistake and spends years living up to it.
tale: Once upon a time, a blacksmith's son named Will was knighted at a ceremony where the king's eyesight had recently declined considerably and the actual recipient of the honour was standing several people to the left. Will, rather than correcting the mistake immediately, said nothing, rode home, and spent the following decade quietly becoming the kind of person who deserved it. By the time anyone thought to check the records he had earned the title three times over by any reasonable measure, and the king, informed of the original error, said he saw no reason to revisit it. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight who is celebrated for a victory that was actually a misunderstanding.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Pol arrived at a border fortress intending to request shelter for the night and was so imposing in full armour, arriving alone at dusk in a thunderstorm, that the garrison assumed he was the advance guard of an army and surrendered without a word being exchanged about conflict. He accepted the surrender with a straight face, sent word to the king, and spent a week in the surrendered fortress eating their food and composing a version of events that was accurate in all its details and misleading in their arrangement. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight whose horse is considerably wiser than anyone gives it credit for.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Bram rode a horse named Dun who had an exceptional ability to stop at exactly the right moment — at ambushes, at unstable bridges, at crossroads where the wrong choice had been made — and who communicated this by simply becoming immovable, which Bram had learned after three years to treat as reliable intelligence. Dun was never wrong. Bram won several campaigns largely by trusting a horse, which he mentioned in his memoirs only in the most oblique terms, but the dedication read: to Dun, who knew. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a princess who solves a kingdom's problems not through magic or marriage but through extremely good record-keeping.
tale: Once upon a time, a princess named Ren discovered that the kingdom's perpetual shortage of grain was not a shortage at all but a bookkeeping error spanning forty years, in which the eastern harvest had been consistently recorded under the wrong province. She spent three months cross-referencing ledgers, corrected the error, redistributed the surplus, and presented her findings to the council in a report so thorough and so clearly formatted that they had no grounds for dispute. The kingdom was solvent for the first time in a generation and she asked for nothing except a better filing system. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a princess who refuses every suitor not out of stubbornness but because she is already in love with a very specific kind of morning.
tale: Once upon a time, a princess named Sela turned down fourteen proposals over seven years, not from fickleness but because she was genuinely content with the hours between five and seven in the morning when the palace was quiet and the light came through her window at an angle that made everything look like the beginning of something. She eventually married a lighthouse keeper who kept similar hours and shared her opinion of them entirely, and they spent many excellent mornings in agreeable silence looking at the same light from different directions. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a princess who goes on a quest disguised as a merchant's daughter and finds the disguise fits her better than the crown.
tale: Once upon a time, a princess named Mira left the palace dressed as a merchant's daughter to find a stolen treaty and discovered over three months of travel that she was considerably better at being a merchant's daughter than a princess: she haggled well, slept soundly on inn mattresses, made friends easily without the performance of rank, and arrived at her destination having enjoyed the journey more than any event the court had ever staged. She returned the treaty, resumed the crown, and spent the rest of her reign making the palace slightly more like the road. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a princess who discovers she can speak to birds and uses the skill to become the kingdom's finest intelligence gatherer.
tale: Once upon a time, a princess named Bea discovered at the age of nine that she understood birds and spent the following thirty years building a network of avian informants that covered every corner of the kingdom and several adjacent ones. She never explained her sources, which the council found frustrating and the king found extremely convenient, and by the time she took the throne she knew more about the kingdom's actual state than any monarch in its history. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a princess who is sent away to be educated and returns so changed that the court does not recognise what to do with her.
tale: Once upon a time, a princess named Ivy was sent at twelve to study with scholars in a distant city and returned at twenty-two with opinions, questions, three languages, and a complete absence of the decorative qualities the court had expected to develop in her absence. She made several courtiers deeply uncomfortable and the king, her father, quietly delighted, and she spent a decade reforming the legal system before anyone had quite worked out how to stop her. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a princess who inherits a kingdom in ruins and spends her reign making it merely difficult.
tale: Once upon a time, a princess named Ora inherited a kingdom with an empty treasury, a flooded capital, and a foreign debt nobody had thought to mention before the coronation. She did not panic, which surprised everyone, and she did not make promises, which surprised them further. She made a list, prioritised ruthlessly, asked for help from places her predecessors had considered beneath them, and by the end of her first decade the kingdom was no longer in ruins but was genuinely difficult, which she considered a solid foundation. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a princess who is famous throughout the kingdom for never once saying anything she did not mean.
tale: Once upon a time, a princess named Sol had the reputation — unusual among royalty — of being entirely literal. She did not pay compliments she did not mean, did not offer reassurance she did not feel, and did not promise what she could not deliver. Courtiers found this initially alarming and eventually indispensable, since a reassurance from her was worth considerably more than a declaration from anyone else. She ruled for forty years and was trusted absolutely, which she found was the most practical thing a reputation could be. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about twin princesses who are mistaken for each other their entire lives and eventually stop correcting people.
tale: Once upon a time, twin princesses named Fen and Flo were so identical that even their parents occasionally confused them, and they discovered in childhood that the confusion gave them a rare freedom: each could inhabit the other's duties when circumstances demanded, and over decades they had effectively shared one role so completely that they could not have said with certainty which of them had done what. When they were old and people asked which was which, they looked at each other, shrugged, and said it had never seemed important enough to clarify. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a princess who builds something with her hands for the first time and discovers a talent nobody expected.
tale: Once upon a time, a princess named Dara was stranded for a week in a forest after her escort became lost and, out of necessity, built a shelter from fallen branches that was so structurally sound it was still standing when a woodcutter found it twelve years later and moved his tools into it. She returned to the palace with a new interest that no one at court quite knew how to incorporate into official duties, and spent her subsequent years designing bridges, which she found considerably more satisfying than being decorative. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a princess who is cursed to always know what people are thinking and the unexpected burden of it.
tale: Once upon a time, a princess named Cas was cursed at birth to hear the thoughts of everyone near her, which was presented at the time as a gift. By the time she was twenty she was exhausted and had developed a strong preference for the company of animals and librarians, who were, in her experience, the most reliably honest thinkers available. She eventually found that the curse had made her the most empathetic ruler in the kingdom's history, provided she could sleep, which she managed on a strict schedule and a great deal of chamomile. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a princess who goes to the market every week in disguise not to spy on her subjects but simply because she likes markets.
tale: Once upon a time, a princess named Lara had been going to the weekly market in a plain coat and a wide hat since she was fourteen because she genuinely loved markets: the noise, the produce, the way the same stall looked different in every season, and the conversations that happened between people who were not performing for anyone. After twenty years the market traders all knew perfectly well who she was and played along because they liked her and she always paid fairly, and the charade satisfied everyone. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a princess who is sent on a diplomatic mission and accidentally negotiates a better treaty than her father the king had managed in ten years.
tale: Once upon a time, a princess named Wren was sent to a neighbouring kingdom as a gesture of goodwill while actual negotiations were conducted by officials, and sat in on the formal talks out of boredom, asked three questions that no one had thought to ask, and by the third day had accidentally identified a compromise that collapsed the central disagreement and produced a treaty both sides signed with relief and only moderate embarrassment that it had taken a bored seventeen-year-old to find it. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a princess who spends her childhood listening at doors and becomes the most informed person in the kingdom.
tale: Once upon a time, a princess named Rue discovered at the age of six that adults spoke freely when they assumed no one important was nearby and spent her childhood in a systematic program of listening at doors, behind curtains, and around corners that gave her, by the time she was eighteen, a more complete picture of the kingdom's actual operations than the entire council possessed. She used this knowledge quietly, strategically, and almost always for good, and the few who eventually understood how she knew what she knew found it more impressive than threatening. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a princess who discovers the palace has a room nobody knew about and what she finds inside it.
tale: Once upon a time, a princess named Brix found a door behind a tapestry in the east wing that opened into a room containing three hundred years of correspondence between every previous occupant of her room and a scholar whose identity changed each generation. The letters were funny, anxious, brilliant, and entirely private, and reading them gave her a sense of continuity with the people who had sat in the same space feeling the same things that she had not expected to find in a palace full of portraits. She wrote her own first letter the same afternoon. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a princess who would rather be a naturalist and finds a way to be both.
tale: Once upon a time, a princess named Sora spent every moment she could in the forest cataloguing insects, plants, and small mammals, and spent every other moment in the throne room wishing she were in the forest. After a decade of this she simply combined the roles: she held audiences outdoors when weather permitted, consulted on boundary disputes by walking the terrain personally, and commissioned the kingdom's first natural history survey as an act of governance rather than personal interest. The kingdom benefited and she was significantly happier, which she had been told would not happen. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a princess who falls asleep for a hundred years and wakes to find the kingdom managed perfectly well without her.
tale: Once upon a time, a princess named Eld fell into an enchanted sleep that lasted a hundred years and woke to find the kingdom prosperous, the roads in good repair, and a constitutional council that had been governing efficiently since the seventieth year when everyone had agreed waiting was impractical. She felt a complicated set of emotions about this and then decided the most useful thing to do was congratulate everyone and find out where she could help, which turned out to be quite a lot of places once she stopped expecting to be needed as a figurehead. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a princess who writes letters to every person who writes to the palace and the unexpected correspondence that results.
tale: Once upon a time, a princess named Pia made it her private project to personally answer every letter sent to the palace, which numbered in the hundreds per month. She wrote briefly but genuinely, asking one real question in each reply, and the resulting correspondence taught her more about the kingdom's actual daily life than any tour or report had managed. Several of the correspondents became advisors. One became a very good friend. The postal service complained about the volume and she paid them extra, which she considered a reasonable administration cost. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a princess who is the only person in the kingdom who can fix the magical clock that keeps the seasons in order.
tale: Once upon a time, a princess named Zoe inherited alongside her throne the maintenance of a vast magical clock buried in the palace cellars that regulated the kingdom's seasons and required adjustment every seven years by someone who understood both its mechanism and its mood, which was considerable. Every previous ruler had employed a clockmaker for the purpose, but Zoe found she had a natural aptitude and preferred to do it herself, spending three days in the cellar every seven years with tools, patience, and complete silence while the kingdom held its breath above her and the seasons waited politely. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a princess who is exiled and spends ten years building something that changes the kingdom without her being in it.
tale: Once upon a time, a princess named Gray was exiled at twenty-three following a court dispute and spent ten years in a small northern town where she built a school, a mill, a system of drainage that doubled the town's agricultural output, and a reputation as someone who solved problems without expecting credit. When the political situation in the capital resolved itself and she returned, she found the ideas she had tested in the north had spread through the kingdom ahead of her, anonymously, and the people who had adopted them did not know where they had come from, which she found entirely satisfying. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a princess who is tasked with breaking an ancient curse and discovers the curse was placed by one of her own ancestors on themselves.
tale: Once upon a time, a princess named Cora was tasked with breaking a curse that had kept the palace gardens frozen in winter for two hundred years and traced it, through considerable archival research, to a great-great-grandmother who had placed it on herself as a form of permanent mourning after a loss the family had never recorded. Breaking the curse required understanding it, and understanding it required reading between the lines of everything the official history had left out, and by the time she spoke the words that ended it she felt she knew her ancestor more honestly than anyone had in two centuries. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight and a princess who are sent to negotiate with the same dragon and discover they have entirely opposite strategies.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Tor and a princess named Ren were sent separately by the same king to negotiate with the same dragon, neither knowing the other had been dispatched, and arrived at the mountain on the same afternoon to find the dragon mildly amused and considerably better informed about the kingdom's internal politics than either of them had expected. Tor wanted to appeal to honour. Ren wanted to appeal to self-interest. The dragon said both approaches had merit and proposed a solution that incorporated elements of each, which it had worked out before they arrived. The treaty lasted two hundred years. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a princess who rescues a knight from a dungeon and the awkward journey home that follows.
tale: Once upon a time, a princess named Sela broke a knight named Cob out of a foreign dungeon using a stolen key, a bribed guard, and considerable nerve, and the two spent three days crossing hostile territory during which Cob was grateful, embarrassed, and unable to decide in what proportion. He offered to carry her pack. She declined. He offered to scout ahead. She said she had already memorised the route. By the time they reached the border they had established a working friendship based on the mutual understanding that competence required no particular explanation. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a princess and a knight who have been rivals since childhood and must complete a quest together.
tale: Once upon a time, a princess named Ivy and a knight named Ash had been competing since they were seven — in lessons, in archery, in who could ride faster and argue better — and were sent on a joint quest as adults by a king who had run out of patience with their inability to be in the same room without a scorecard. The quest required both of them to succeed simultaneously, which they achieved on the fourth day through a solution neither would have found alone, and they returned home with the uncomfortable knowledge that the other person was the most useful rival they had ever had. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight and a princess who swap roles for a week as part of a bet and discover something true about each other.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Gray and a princess named Mira made a bet — she would spend a week in training and he would spend a week in audiences and correspondence — and both found, by the end of it, that the other's work was harder than it looked and more varied than they had assumed. Gray wrote twelve letters in one morning and had opinions about fabric by Thursday. Mira could not hold a sword with one hand and thought this was the most honest week of her adult life. The bet was declared a draw, which meant both of them had lost, which meant both of them had won. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a princess and a knight who are the only two people in the kingdom who know a particular secret.
tale: Once upon a time, a princess named Bea and a knight named Fen were the only two people who knew that the kingdom's famous enchanted sword was not enchanted at all, having been the two who had accidentally demagicked it during an unsupervised experiment twelve years earlier and then said nothing. The sword's reputation had won four battles in the interim, which they had discussed in private with increasing complexity of feeling, and they had developed, as a result, a particular friendship based on shared guilt, shared discretion, and the mutual understanding that some secrets are too useful to confess. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a princess and a knight who write letters to each other for twenty years before they meet in person.
