tale: Once upon a time, a princess named Sol and a knight named Rue began exchanging letters at the age of fourteen through a mutual friend and wrote to each other for twenty years — through campaigns and coronations, through grief and celebration — before circumstances finally brought them to the same court. They recognised each other immediately and spent the first evening in comfortable conversation that felt, as Rue said later, like continuing a sentence rather than beginning one. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight who is assigned to protect a princess and discovers she is considerably more dangerous than anything assigned to threaten her.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Pol was assigned to protect a princess named Wren from a series of threats that turned out, upon investigation, to have been dispatched by Wren herself in order to test the quality of her guard. Pol was the first to pass all three tests, which Wren acknowledged by telling him this directly, which he found more alarming than the tests. He stayed for nine years and said it was the only assignment that had kept him genuinely attentive. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a princess and a knight who are sent on the same quest without being told the other has been sent.
tale: Once upon a time, a princess named Dara and a knight named Eli were each privately commissioned by the same adviser to retrieve the same stolen map, for entirely different reasons neither had disclosed. They met at the thief's house, each having arrived from a different direction, and spent an uncomfortable ten minutes establishing that the adviser had played them both before deciding the most productive response was to complete the task together and present a united front of irritation upon their return. The map was retrieved. The adviser was reassigned. The two remained allies. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about an old knight and a young princess who travel together and teach each other something the other could not have learned alone.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Ash who had fought for forty years and a princess named Calla who had been alive for sixteen were thrown together by a border emergency that required them to travel three days through unfamiliar territory. Ash taught her to read the land and sleep in any conditions and say only what needed saying. She taught him that admitting uncertainty to someone younger than yourself was not a loss of authority but an extension of it. Both of them arrived changed in ways they found difficult to articulate but did not forget. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a princess who trains secretly as a knight and the knight who discovers her secret and says nothing.
tale: Once upon a time, a princess named Lira had been training in the early mornings with borrowed equipment in a far courtyard for two years when a knight named Dom came to the courtyard to think before dawn and found her in the middle of a drill sequence that was considerably better than most of his new recruits. He watched for a moment, nodded once, and left without a word. He did not mention it, and she did not ask why he had not, and the following year when a position opened in the guard she was qualified for it in every way that could be verified. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight and a princess who disagree about everything except what actually matters.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Cas and a princess named Ora disagreed about strategy, protocol, horses, the proper temperature of a room, the correct interpretation of three separate historical events, and whether soup counted as a meal. They agreed, without ever discussing it explicitly, on questions of fairness, on when to speak and when to be quiet, on which promises should be kept at cost, and on the importance of not lying to people who were counting on you. The council found them exhausting. Their subjects found them reliable. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a princess and a knight who are lost in a forest and spend three days arguing about which direction is north.
tale: Once upon a time, a princess named Rue and a knight named Bram were lost in the same forest for three days due to a mutual navigational disagreement that neither would concede and both were wrong about. On the morning of the fourth day they found a road by following a stream, having simultaneously and independently arrived at this correct approach without acknowledging that the other had suggested it a day earlier. The debate about which direction had been north continued intermittently for the next twenty years and became the thing their children most enjoyed listening to. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight who is famous for honesty and a princess who is famous for tact and the impossible situation that requires both.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Sol who never softened bad news and a princess named Pia who never delivered it without softening were jointly asked to inform a neighbouring king that his trusted general was a traitor. They spent an evening working out an approach that was both completely honest and survivable for the messenger, and arrived at a version that was accurate, carefully sequenced, and concluded before the king's temper had time to redirect itself toward them. The general was arrested. They were thanked. They agreed on the way home that collaboration had produced a better outcome than either method alone. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a princess and a knight who are both pretending to be someone else at the same inn.
tale: Once upon a time, a princess named Zoe travelling under a merchant's name and a knight named Wick travelling under a farmer's name ended up at the same inn in the same province on the same night and spent an enjoyable evening each convinced the other was exactly who they claimed to be, which gave both of them considerable freedom to say what they actually thought about several matters of mutual interest. They compared notes honestly in the morning, when the disguises became impossible to maintain, and found that honesty in disguise had told them more about each other than any formal introduction would have. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight who has given up questing and a princess who has just decided to begin.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Eld who had returned from his last quest and hung up his armour with considerable relief met a princess named Cora who had just packed her first saddlebag with considerable excitement, and the two of them spent an afternoon on the same bench in the same garden at different stages of the same journey. He told her what the road was actually like and she told him what she hoped to find, and neither tried to change the other's mind, and both of them left the conversation slightly better prepared for what was coming next. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a princess and a knight who are assigned to write the official history of a battle and cannot agree on a single fact.
tale: Once upon a time, a princess named Ren and a knight named Tor were jointly tasked with writing the official account of a battle both had witnessed from different positions and discovered they had, between them, two complete and entirely incompatible versions of events that were each internally consistent and mutually exclusive. They eventually produced a document with two columns that the archivist accepted with the observation that it was the most honest military history the kingdom had ever produced, which they received as a compliment only after thinking about it for a while. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight who falls under a sleeping curse and a princess who stays beside him not because it will wake him but because leaving seems wrong.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Ash fell under a sleeping curse in a foreign castle and a princess named Sela who had been travelling with him stayed beside him not because she had any means of breaking the curse but because she had read enough to know that people who woke alone often woke confused and frightened and that having a familiar face nearby cost her nothing and might matter. He woke on the fifteenth day, saw her in the chair beside him, and said nothing for a long time. She said she had been reading. He said he knew. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a princess and a knight who build something together that neither could have built alone.
tale: Once upon a time, a princess named Mira who understood engineering and a knight named Fen who understood people spent two years designing and then building a bridge across the river that had divided the kingdom's two halves for a century. She designed the structure. He managed the workers, the politics, the funding disputes, and the two floods that threatened to undo the first season's work. On the day the bridge opened and the first cart crossed, they stood at either end and neither of them could have said where their contribution ended and the other's began. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight who protects a princess from an enemy and a princess who protects the knight from the same enemy by different means.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Gray placed himself between a princess named Ivy and a political enemy who had arrived with armed guards, and while Gray was holding the physical ground Ivy was dismantling the enemy's legal claim, his funding arrangements, and his alliance with the eastern province in a conversation conducted entirely in the ten minutes it took the situation to resolve itself. The enemy left without the guards having raised a sword. Gray said afterward that he had not been entirely certain which of them had done more protecting. Ivy said the answer was obvious. Neither agreed on which answer that was. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a princess and a knight who have been friends since childhood and the day they realise how unusual that is.
tale: Once upon a time, a princess named Bea and a knight named Eli had been friends since they were five and it had never occurred to either of them that this was unusual until a foreign dignitary expressed considerable surprise at seeing them argue cheerfully about a tactical problem across the same table with the same amount of authority. Bea explained they had grown up together. The dignitary said that was not how it was done in his country. They looked at each other and then back at him and decided they preferred their way and changed the subject. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight and a dragon who have been fighting the same battle for so long that neither can remember who started it.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Tor and a dragon named Vel had been fighting in the same valley every third Tuesday for eleven years, which both of them had come to treat as a standing appointment. On the hundred and forty-third occasion Tor arrived to find Vel sitting rather than standing and Vel admitted she could not actually remember the original cause of the conflict. Tor could not either. They sat together for an hour trying to reconstruct it, failed, and agreed that the absence of a reason was itself a sufficient reason to stop. They shook hand and claw and went home. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight who is tasked with slaying a dragon and arrives to find the dragon is dying of old age anyway.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Ash rode three days to slay a dragon who turned out to be approximately nine hundred years old and in the process of dying with considerable dignity in a large cave full of things she had collected across the centuries. She offered him tea, which he accepted, and they spent the afternoon talking about the places she had seen and the things she had outlived, and when he rode home he reported that the dragon situation had been resolved, which was accurate, and said nothing further, which felt right. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a dragon who saves a knight's life and the complicated debt that follows.
tale: Once upon a time, a dragon named Cass pulled a knight named Bram out of a flooded ravine during a rainstorm, having no particular reason to do so except that she had been passing and the alternative seemed worse. Bram spent the following month trying to discharge the debt in ways the dragon found increasingly unnecessary: he brought her a sheep (she did not eat sheep anymore), repaired her cave entrance (she had liked the gap), and wrote her name in a book of heroic deeds (she found the category inapplicable). She eventually told him the debt was cleared when he stopped trying to settle it, which he found philosophically challenging but accepted. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight and a dragon who are both lost in the same forest and reluctantly agree to navigate together.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Ren and a dragon named Roo were both lost in the same ancient forest, having arrived from opposite sides and become turned around within the hour. They encountered each other at a stream and spent several minutes deciding whether to be adversaries or resources, and concluded, practically, that two sets of eyes and a superior aerial view were worth the awkwardness. The dragon scouted from above and the knight read the ground, and they found the edge of the forest by late afternoon and parted with a nod that both found unexpectedly cordial. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight who befriends a dragon by being the first person to ask her what she actually wants.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Sol arrived at a dragon's mountain with her sword sheathed and her first question — which was what the dragon actually wanted, not what the kingdom assumed she wanted — produced a long silence followed by the most honest conversation the dragon had had in four centuries. What she wanted was quite specific and entirely reasonable and had never once been asked about. Sol returned to the king with a proposal rather than a trophy and the negotiation took two weeks and produced a lasting agreement. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a young knight who is terrified of dragons and an old dragon who is tired of being feared.
tale: Once upon a time, a young knight named Mira who was genuinely, physically terrified of dragons was sent as a messenger to the nearest one, a very old dragon named Eld who had spent two centuries being regarded with dread and found it, by this point, exhausting. They were therefore unusually well matched: she was too frightened to perform bravery and he was too tired to perform menace, and they had a short, honest, slightly undignified conversation that resolved the message's purpose immediately and left both of them in better spirits than they had arrived in. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight and a dragon who are forced to share a shelter during a blizzard.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Gray and a dragon named Flint were caught in the same mountain blizzard at the same time and both took shelter in the same cave, which was just large enough for both of them if they did not move unnecessarily. The dragon provided warmth. The knight provided company, which he offered in the form of three stories and a card game he taught her using a deck from his saddlebag. The blizzard lasted two days. They emerged with an arrangement by which the dragon would send word if she ever needed a messenger and the knight would send word if he ever needed a warm cave. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight and a dragon who discover they were both deceived by the same person.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Paz and a dragon named Ora were both manipulated by the same adviser into threatening each other over the same territory, which neither actually wanted but which both had been told the other was planning to seize. They discovered the deception by accident when their paths crossed on neutral ground and each described their version of events and found the stories were mirror images. They presented themselves jointly to the king, which the adviser had not anticipated, and the situation resolved itself with a clarity that required no swords and no fire and left the adviser in a considerably reduced position. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a retired knight who becomes a dragon's unlikely companion in old age.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Dom retired at sixty-three to a small house on the edge of a mountain and discovered that the mountain had a dragon, a very old one named Ash, who turned out to be an excellent neighbour: quiet, consistent, not given to small talk, and appreciative of being left alone except on the evenings when company was welcome. They developed a routine across several years in which each knew when the other wanted to talk and when they did not, and Dom said in his last years it was the most comfortable friendship of his life, which he attributed to the dragon's three centuries of practice. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight and a dragon who enter the same competition and find themselves working together in the final round.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Eli and a dragon named Flo both entered a regional problem-solving competition — the knight for the prize and the dragon out of genuine interest in the problem — and advanced independently to the final round, which required two competitors to work together on a challenge neither could complete alone. They looked at each other across the competition floor and made a rapid and entirely practical agreement, solved the problem in forty minutes, and split the prize in a ratio they worked out on the way home. They entered the following year's competition as a registered team. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a dragon who asks a knight for protection and the knight who must decide what that means.
tale: Once upon a time, a dragon named Sela arrived at a knight's door and asked for protection, which was unusual enough that the knight, Fen, took several minutes to answer. The dragon explained that a group of hunters had been pursuing her for weeks and that she had run out of mountains to retreat to. Fen considered the various implications of sheltering a dragon from hunters in a kingdom that generally considered dragons property of the crown and decided the question of what was right was clearer than the question of what was convenient. He took the case. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight and a dragon who are jointly assigned to guard the same thing and must negotiate custody.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Cora and a dragon named Wren were both commissioned to guard the kingdom's most important archive — the knight by the council and the dragon by an older agreement nobody had remembered to rescind — and spent the first week establishing a schedule that neither found perfect but both found workable. The archive was the most thoroughly guarded building in the kingdom's history and also the most unusual, and visitors often stopped outside simply to see a knight and a dragon sharing a post with the studied mutual tolerance of two professionals who had decided to be excellent at something that surprised everyone. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight who trains with a dragon and becomes the finest fighter in the kingdom.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Rue spent three years training with a dragon named Sol who had been watching human combat for five centuries and had several hundred years' worth of opinions about it. The dragon's instruction was unconventional, frequently uncomfortable, and entirely accurate, and the knight emerged from the training able to read a situation faster and respond to it more accurately than anyone who had trained only against other humans. She won every tournament she entered and attributed it in official records to dedicated practice, which was true, and said nothing about her instructor, which was a separate conversation entirely. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight who is turned into a dragon and the dragon who helps her understand what she has become.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Ivy was turned into a dragon by a curse she had walked into without sufficient caution and spent her first three days as a large, confused, accidentally destructive presence in a forest until a dragon named Cas found her and explained, with patience and without condescension, the basic practicalities of being what she now was. The curse was eventually broken, but Ivy said afterward that the three days had been the most disorienting and the most educational of her life, and she treated dragons differently from then on, which she considered the curse's one useful consequence. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a dragon who has been waiting for a knight to arrive for a hundred years and what happens when one finally does.
tale: Once upon a time, a dragon named Eld had been waiting beside a sealed door in a mountain for a hundred years because a prophecy had told her a knight would come who could open it, and she had taken the prophecy seriously enough to stay. The knight who finally arrived, a young woman named Bex, opened the door by pushing it, which had apparently always been unlocked, and the dragon looked at the door and then at the knight and then at the room behind the door, which contained a modest but useful collection of historical documents, and decided that a hundred years of faithful waiting still felt like the right thing to have done. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight and a dragon who are the last two members of an order that has otherwise entirely disbanded.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Pol and a dragon named Dara were the only remaining members of the Order of the Grey Road, an ancient institution whose other members had retired, died, or simply stopped showing up over the previous fifty years. They met once a year on the order's founding date to conduct the traditional ceremonies, which took about twenty minutes, and then usually stayed for dinner and talked about everything the order had originally been created to protect, which was still worth protecting and which they both continued to do individually regardless of whether anyone was counting. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight and a dragon who solve a mystery that has puzzled the kingdom for fifty years.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Gray who was good at finding patterns and a dragon named Vel who had been alive during the original events collaborated on the kingdom's oldest cold case: the disappearance of an entire village on a single night fifty years before. The dragon provided memory and the knight provided method, and together they established within a month that the village had not disappeared but had relocated voluntarily and was now a prosperous settlement two valleys over operating under a different name. The kingdom updated its records. The village expressed mild relief. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a dragon who teaches a knight to read the landscape and a knight who teaches a dragon to read people.
tale: Once upon a time, a dragon named Ora and a knight named Sev each had a skill the other lacked entirely. Ora could read a landscape — its history, its water, its safety — in a way the knight could not begin to do. Sev could read a room of people — their alliances, their fears, their actual intentions — in a way the dragon found impossible. They spent a year travelling together, each teaching the other their particular fluency, and arrived at the end of it able to navigate both the land and the people on it with a competence that neither had possessed at the start. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight who refuses to fight a dragon and the unexpected outcome of that refusal.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Lira arrived at a dragon's mountain, assessed the situation, and put her sword away, deciding that the dragon had done nothing that justified a fight and that she was not willing to begin one on someone else's behalf without a better reason than tradition. The dragon, who had been preparing for a fight out of habit, spent a confused moment recalibrating and then invited the knight to sit down. What followed was a negotiation that produced an agreement neither kingdom had thought possible and that Lira's superiors received with mixed feelings and eventually accepted. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a knight and a dragon who raise an orphaned creature together and discover they are better caregivers than either expected.
tale: Once upon a time, a knight named Bram and a dragon named Cass found an orphaned gryphon chick in the wreckage of a storm and were jointly responsible for it before either had agreed to be, the chick having attached itself to both of them with the absolute certainty of something that has decided. They were an unlikely household and a surprisingly effective one: the dragon provided warmth and the occasional stern word, the knight provided structure and the more frequent stern word, and the gryphon grew up confident, well-fed, and entirely unbothered by the unusualness of its family. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who collects questions instead of answers and what his collection eventually teaches him.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Arc kept a notebook in which he wrote only questions, never answers, because he had noticed that answers tended to close things while questions kept them open. By the time he was grown the notebook had four hundred and twelve questions in it, ranging from the very small to the very large, and reading it back he found that the questions together formed a more accurate portrait of who he had been at each age than any answer could have provided. He started a second notebook. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who can speak to rivers and what the rivers tell him about the land.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Bram discovered he could understand rivers — not as words exactly, but as a continuous report of everywhere the water had been and everything it had passed through. He learned about distant floods from the urgency of a local stream, learned about drought three valleys over from the way a brook seemed to be rationing itself, and learned about a lost village from the particular sadness in a river that still flowed past the place where the buildings had once stood. He became a mapmaker whose maps were better than anyone else's and he never explained why. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who is the only person in his village who is not afraid of the forest and what he finds there.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Sev grew up in a village that treated the forest at its edge as a place of danger, and he was the only one who had ever walked into it and come back saying it was simply a forest: dark in places, full of things living their own lives, neither hostile nor welcoming but entirely indifferent in the way that large old things tend to be. He went in regularly and came back with mushrooms and occasionally useful information, and the village gradually stopped being afraid of the forest and started sending him with shopping lists. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who befriends the oldest man in the village and what the old man gives him that cannot be held.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Eli spent his afternoons with the oldest man in the village, a man named Wick who was ninety-four and had outlived every person he had originally known. Wick did not give Eli things. He gave him time in the form of unhurried attention, and in that time he described the world as it had been before the boy existed, which gave the boy a sense of living inside something much larger than his own experience. When Wick died, Eli sat quietly for a long time and then wrote down everything he could remember, which was most of it. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who makes friends with a shadow that is not his own.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Cob noticed that the shadow beside him on a sunny afternoon was not shaped like him and did not move when he moved, but sat quietly and seemed, in the way shadows can seem things, to be waiting. He tried talking to it and it did not answer but it stayed, and over the following weeks he came to understand it as company of a particular kind: present, faithful, requiring nothing. His mother said it was simply a trick of the light. He said that might be true and did not change his opinion of it. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who is raised by his grandmother and learns that her knowledge is older than any book.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Tor was raised by his grandmother in a house full of practical knowledge that had never been written down: which plants to use for which ailments, which weather signs to trust, which silences meant something and which meant nothing. He did well in school but said later that the most useful education of his life had happened in his grandmother's kitchen, and when she died he spent two years writing down everything he could remember, which was published as a small book that was used by people who knew exactly what it was for. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who can hear music in everything and the composer he eventually becomes.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Finn heard music in things that made no sound to anyone else: the rhythm of a door closing, the pitch of wind through a specific gap, the interval between two people's footsteps when they were walking in agreement. He wrote these down in a notation system he invented himself and grew into a composer whose work people described as sounding like the world explaining itself. He said he had not invented anything, only listened more carefully than most. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who grows a garden in an abandoned lot that transforms the street around it.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Rue planted seeds in the abandoned lot between two houses on his street because he had seeds and it was empty and no one had said he could not. By the end of summer the lot was full of sunflowers and tomatoes and one enormous pumpkin that became the subject of considerable local discussion, and by the following spring the two neighbours on either side had started their own small gardens, and by the third year the street had a reputation for flowers that brought people from other streets to walk along it. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who is afraid of the dark until he learns what lives in it.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Dom was afraid of the dark until his uncle, who was an astronomer, took him to a hilltop on a clear night and showed him what the dark actually contained: more light than the day ever showed, only light from so far away it had been travelling for longer than anyone could easily think about. Dom was not immediately unafraid but he was immediately interested, which was different and better, and by the time he was grown he had stopped being afraid of anything he had taken the time to understand. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who discovers his father's old journals and the person they reveal.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Gray found his father's journals in a trunk in the attic, written in the years before his father had become his father, and discovered inside them a person who had been uncertain, funny, wrong about several things, right about others, and genuinely trying. The journals did not make his father easier to understand but they made him easier to love, which the boy had not expected and which he thought about for many years. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who builds a boat and teaches himself to sail on a lake that everyone says is too small to matter.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Sol built a boat from salvaged wood and taught himself to sail on the small lake at the edge of his town, which was so unremarkable that no one else had ever bothered to learn it as a body of water. He knew every current and shallowing and how the wind came off the eastern hill in the afternoon, and the discipline and patience of learning a small thing thoroughly turned out to be excellent preparation for the larger waters he sailed later in life. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who tells stories to his younger siblings every night and what the habit makes of him.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Eli told his three younger siblings a story every night before sleep, invented entirely on the spot from whatever had happened that day. He was not particularly skilled at first and some of the early stories did not have endings, but by the time the youngest sibling was old enough to not need bedtime stories anymore Eli had told over a thousand and could begin a story from almost any starting point and find his way to a satisfying conclusion. He wrote his first book the following year and said later it had taken him twelve years of nightly practice to produce something that looked like it had come easily. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who runs away from home and is found by the home he was actually looking for.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Fen ran away at thirteen not from his home exactly but toward something he could not name, walked two days through countryside he had never seen, and arrived at a farm where an elderly couple with no children took him in for the night and then the week and then the season, not by pressing him to stay but simply by having the quality of life he had been looking for and not knowing it. He wrote to his parents and they visited and everyone understood the arrangement in different ways and none of those ways were wrong. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who learns his town's history by asking the oldest buildings what they remember.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Cas discovered that old buildings, if you sat quietly inside them long enough, seemed to give off the accumulated weight of their histories — a particular quality of silence in a room where a great deal had happened, a warmth in a wall that had been a bakery for a hundred years, an echo in a courtyard that felt like it was still resonating with voices. He could not explain this and did not try to, but he used it to write a history of his town that the local archivist said was the most accurate account ever produced, all without a single document. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who is extraordinarily patient and what his patience eventually earns him.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Will was patient in a way that distinguished him from every other child his age: patient with difficult tasks, patient with difficult people, patient with situations that seemed to have no resolution. He did not wait passively — he paid attention while he waited — and by the time he was an adult he had a reputation for being the person you sent to situations that had defeated everyone else's speed and urgency, and the situations usually resolved in his presence as though they too had been waiting for someone willing to give them sufficient time. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who is the only one in his family who cannot do magic and what he does instead.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Pol grew up in a family where every member could perform some form of magic except him, which had been established early and handled kindly and was simply a fact. He developed instead an extremely precise understanding of how magic worked, what its limits were, and where the gaps between magical solutions and the problems those solutions left behind could be filled by someone paying close attention without any particular power. He became the most sought-after advisor in the kingdom, which his family found entirely appropriate. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who keeps a lighthouse alone for one summer and what the solitude teaches him.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Roo spent a summer alone in a lighthouse keeping the light while the regular keeper recovered from an illness, and the solitude, which he had expected to find difficult, turned out to be simply a different kind of company: the sea and the light and the rhythm of the shifts and the passage of ships he would never know. He returned home with a clearer sense of who he was when no one else was defining him by their expectations, which he found was a useful thing to know early. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who befriends the new child in the village when no one else will.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Bex was the first to speak to the new child who arrived mid-year and spoke differently and wore different clothes and was being watched from a careful distance by everyone else. He did not do this out of particular nobility but out of curiosity, and the friendship that resulted from that curiosity turned out to be the most interesting friendship of his childhood, which the watching children gradually joined in ways that required no announcement. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who draws maps of places that do not exist and one day finds one of them.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Miro spent his childhood drawing maps of countries he had invented: their coastlines, their rivers, their capital cities with invented names, their mountain ranges with imagined heights. At twenty-three, travelling in a remote region he had never visited, he came over a ridge and saw a valley whose layout matched almost exactly a map he had drawn at the age of nine, including the lake in the western corner shaped like a boot. He sat down on the ridge for a long time. He did not know what to conclude and decided that was all right. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who makes a promise to a tree and keeps it for thirty years.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Dov made a promise to the oak tree at the bottom of his garden — he was seven and entirely sincere — that he would visit it every year on his birthday for as long as he lived in the world. He was thirty-seven when he finally missed a year because of a hospital stay, and he went the day he was released instead, walking slowly with a cane. The tree was considerably larger than when he had made the promise. He was considerably different from the boy who had made it. He thought the promise still counted and counted the visit. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who collects the last words of things that are ending and what she does with them.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Sela had a habit of listening carefully at endings — the last bell of a school year, the final night in a house before a move, the last conversation before something changed — and writing down whatever was said, even if it seemed ordinary. By the time she was grown she had seventeen notebooks of last words that were, taken together, the most honest account of how things actually ended that she had ever read, which was not in triumph or in grief but usually in the middle of something ordinary. She kept writing. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who can see what things were before they became what they are now.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Mira could look at any made thing and see its history layered inside it like rings in a tree: the chair that had been a forest, the bread that had been a field, the road that had been a river valley. She found this mostly beautiful and occasionally overwhelming and learned, over time, to look at things in the ordinary way when she needed to and in the deep way when she had the patience for it. She became a restorer of old objects and her clients said she understood their things more completely than they did themselves. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who is the best navigator anyone has ever met and has never once needed a map.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Rue always knew where she was, which was a quality so natural to her that she had not understood for years that it was unusual. She navigated by a combination of light angle, plant behaviour, air quality, and something she could not name that was simply a sense of where north was the way some people had a sense of time. She became a guide in difficult country and was never once wrong about a route, which she attributed to paying attention, which was true but insufficient as an explanation. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who inherits a library of books that have never been read and what she finds inside them.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Brix inherited a room of books from a great-uncle who had spent his life acquiring volumes he never opened, believing the unread book to be in a state of pure potential. She opened every one over the following two years and found inside them: pressed flowers, margin notes in three languages, a letter that should have been posted, a recipe for something she could not identify, and, in the very last book, a photograph of her great-uncle as a young man looking directly at the camera with an expression she had never seen on his face in life. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who speaks a language nobody else remembers and the grandmother who taught it to her.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Fen spoke a language with her grandmother that no one else in the family understood, a language the grandmother had brought from a place she rarely discussed, and which existed now only in the two of them. When the grandmother died the girl was the last speaker, and she spent twenty years teaching the language to anyone who wanted to learn it, not because she expected it to be saved but because she found that teaching it kept the grandmother present in a way that nothing else managed as completely. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who is raised by a lighthouse and the sea and what she becomes because of it.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Lara grew up in a lighthouse on a small island with her father, and the lighthouse and the sea were the two constants of her childhood: the light requiring maintenance and attention, the sea requiring respect and observation. She grew up knowing how to read weather from water, how to be useful in a confined space, how to keep something running that other people's lives depended on, and how to be alone without being lonely. She became the finest harbour master the region had ever had, which surprised no one who had met her father. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who trains a difficult horse that no one else can ride.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Dara spent six months with a horse that had thrown every rider who had attempted it and was described by the stable master as simply impossible. She did not attempt to ride it at first, only to be near it, and then to be trusted by it, and then to be understood by it, and the riding happened at some point during the fifth month as a natural consequence of everything that had come before. The horse carried her as willingly as any horse she had ridden and she said the six months had been the best education in patience and attention she had received from any teacher, human or otherwise. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who builds clocks and the clock she builds that measures something other than time.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Zoe learned clockmaking from her father and surpassed him by seventeen and built her first original mechanism at nineteen: a clock that did not measure time but distance from the last good thing that had happened, its hand moving slowly away from the moment of joy and requiring a new good thing to reset it. She built one for herself and one for a woman she met who needed it and eventually for anyone who asked, and each one was calibrated slightly differently to the person it was made for, which was the part of the work she found most interesting. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who is the only person in the kingdom who is not impressed by the king.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Ivy was not rude about the king, simply unimpressed, which was a quality so rare at court that the king eventually summoned her to find out what it felt like to speak to someone who was not performing admiration. The conversation was the most useful he had had in years and he hired her to attend council meetings and say, afterward, what she had actually thought, which she did with such complete accuracy that the council began to dread her presence and the king began to depend on it. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who makes friends by asking people what they are proudest of and listening to the answer.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Pia had discovered early that asking people what they were most proud of and listening carefully to the answer was the most reliable way to understand someone she had met. The answers were never what she expected: the powerful were proud of small private things, the quiet were proud of difficult ones, and the people who seemed to have nothing were often proud of exactly the right thing. She became a journalist and said the question had produced better material than any other she had learned. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who grows up in a travelling circus and what she learns from every town the circus passes through.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Cass grew up in a travelling circus and visited two hundred and thirty-seven towns before she was eighteen, never staying more than a week in any of them. She learned that every town had a version of the same arguments, the same alliances, the same people who came to the circus as relief from something they would not name, and the same children who watched the performers with the particular attention of someone who has just understood that the world is larger than they had been told. She eventually wrote all of this down and it was accurate in every town that read it. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who fixes broken things and cannot pass a broken thing without stopping.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Wren could not walk past a broken thing without assessing it: a cracked gate, a torn hem, a bicycle with a flat tyre, a conversation going wrong between two people at the next table. She did not always intervene — she had learned which things could be fixed and which needed to be left — but she was constitutionally incapable of ignoring damage, and the skill this developed in her made her the finest structural engineer of her generation, though she said the most important repairs she had ever made had nothing to do with buildings. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who is sent to live with relatives she has never met and discovers she belongs there.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Ora was sent at twelve to live with relatives she had never met in a place she had never been and expected to be a stranger for years. Instead she found, in the first week, that these people had the same rhythm she had — the same way of being quiet, the same things they found funny, the same threshold for when to speak and when not to — and understood that belonging was sometimes not about familiarity but about frequency, and she had simply found people who operated at the same frequency she did. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who carries her mother's garden with her wherever she goes.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Bea left home at eighteen with a satchel that contained, among other things, a small tin of soil from her mother's garden and seeds from the plants she had most loved growing up. In every place she lived long enough to plant something she put seeds in the soil from the tin and mixed it with the local earth, so that her mother's garden was, after twenty years, distributed across eleven countries and had produced descendants of the original plants in climates it had no business thriving in, which it did anyway. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who is the last person anyone expected to be brave and the first person to act.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Pol was quiet in groups and slow to speak and generally overlooked in situations that were moving quickly, and when the crisis came she was the first to act, not because she had become a different person but because she had been paying attention in a way the louder people had not. She had simply noticed what needed doing before anyone else had finished assessing the situation, and she did it. Afterward she was described as brave, which she found inaccurate and unhelpful, because what she had been was attentive, and she thought that was the more useful lesson. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who writes letters to her future self and one day receives a reply.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Sol wrote letters to her future self every year from the age of nine, sealing them and dating them for ten years ahead, and received one morning at thirty-two a letter in her own handwriting that she had no memory of writing, which said exactly what she needed to hear about a decision she was in the middle of making, in terms specific enough that she could not explain it as coincidence and general enough that she could not prove it was anything else. She kept writing the annual letters and paid more attention to what she said in them than she had before. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who is always cold and the winter she finally finds out why.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Calla had been cold her whole life in a way that had nothing to do with the weather, a persistent interior cold that more blankets did not address, and spent a winter following the feeling rather than fighting it until it led her to a frozen lake at the edge of a forest where the ice, she felt with complete certainty, had been waiting for her. She did not know what this meant and did not need to. She stood on the ice for an hour until the cold felt not like an absence of warmth but like a particular kind of presence, and walked home lighter than she had arrived. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who keeps a record of every kindness she witnesses and what the record reveals.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Ren began keeping a record at thirteen of every act of kindness she witnessed — not grand gestures but ordinary ones: someone holding a door, a stranger passing an umbrella, a child sharing a snack unprompted — and found by the end of the first year that she had recorded two hundred and forty-three separate instances that she would previously have not noticed. The record revealed to her that kindness was not rare but simply unlogged, and she spent the rest of her life paying attention to what was there rather than mourning what seemed absent. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who discovers she can draw what people are dreaming.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Noa woke each morning with clear images in her mind that she eventually understood were not her own dreams but other people's, transmitted somehow during the hours when everyone's defences were down. She drew them carefully in a sketchbook and showed no one for years, and when she finally exhibited the drawings anonymously the people who came to the gallery stood in front of specific images for a very long time with expressions she had learned to read as recognition. She never explained the source and the drawings remained the most honest portraits of the city anyone had made. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who is given one wish and uses it to give someone else two.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Vera was offered one wish by something old she found at a crossroads and spent three days thinking about it before returning to say that she wished for the next person who came to that crossroads to be offered two wishes instead of one. The thing at the crossroads was silent for a moment and then said that was the first time in four hundred years anyone had done that, and it granted the wish, and added, without being asked, that Vera herself could consider her wish partially used up and was therefore owed half of one, which she accepted and used carefully later, at the right moment. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy and a girl who grow up on opposite sides of a river and communicate for years by leaving messages in a bottle.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Bram on the east bank and a girl named Sela on the west bank of a wide river began leaving messages in sealed bottles at the age of eight, after Bram sent one across on a dare and Sela answered it as a matter of principle. They wrote for ten years — about what they could see from their respective sides, what they were reading, what they were afraid of, what they wanted — and when they finally met at a bridge built upstream they recognised each other immediately, which surprised neither of them since they had already said everything important. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy and a girl who are the only two students at a very small school and what they teach each other.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Eli and a girl named Mira were the only two students at a school with one teacher in a village too small to fill a classroom, and they spent nine years sitting on opposite sides of the same table, learning the same lessons at different speeds and in different directions. Eli was quicker with numbers and Mira was quicker with words and each had spent years explaining their particular fluency to the other until both of them were, in a way neither recognised until later, educated by the person sitting across from them as much as by the teacher between them. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy and a girl who find the same lost object on the same day and must work out who it belongs to.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Cob and a girl named Rue each found the same brass compass within an hour of each other in different parts of the same forest, which was impossible and which they could only explain by the compass having moved between findings, though neither had seen it move. They argued its ownership for the better part of an afternoon and eventually concluded that a compass that moved on its own probably knew where it wanted to be, which apparently was with both of them together, and they kept it jointly and used it, thereafter, for every journey either of them took. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy and a girl who build a tree house together and what they discover is possible when neither tries to be in charge.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Fen and a girl named Ivy spent a summer building a tree house in the oak at the back of Ivy's garden, and neither had any more experience than the other, which meant the project went slowly and produced a structure that leaned slightly to the left and had a door that only worked if you lifted it as you pushed, and which they were both more proud of than anything either had made alone because every decision had required both of them to agree on it. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy and a girl who are lost in the same city on the same day and find their way home by helping each other.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Sol and a girl named Bea were both lost in a large unfamiliar city on the same afternoon, having arrived separately and become disoriented independently, and met at a bus stop where both were looking at their phones with the expressions of people whose phones were not helping. Sol knew the northern half of the city and Bea knew the southern half, neither having admitted this immediately, and once they established what each knew they navigated the other home in turn, which took longer than necessary but considerably less time than going alone. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy and a girl who make a bet about something small and find it has unexpectedly large consequences.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Tor and a girl named Cas made a bet at ten years old about whether a particular seed would grow into a tree by the time they were twenty, and tended the seedling competitively for years — each watering it when the other had not, each tracking its height, each invested in an outcome that had begun as a wager and become, by degrees, a shared project they were both embarrassed to admit they cared about. The tree was eight metres tall when they turned twenty and neither of them could say who had won because neither of them had kept the original terms. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy and a girl who are the best of friends until they are teenagers and must find their way back to each other.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Dom and a girl named Lara were inseparable until they were thirteen, at which point the ordinary pressures of being thirteen pulled them into different orbits for four years during which they were politely distant in the way of people who know each other too well to be strangers and too awkwardly to be close. At seventeen they were put in the same group for a school project and spent three weeks remembering why they had been friends, which turned out to require less effort than either had feared. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy and a girl who both want the same thing and discover they can only get it by wanting it together.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Wick and a girl named Ora both wanted the same plot in the community garden and arrived at the council meeting prepared to argue their respective cases, and each made such a thorough and genuine argument that the committee awarded the plot jointly, which neither had wanted and both came to accept. The garden they grew together was better than either had planned separately, the complementary things they each knew about soil and water and what to plant next to what producing a result that could not have come from one person's vision alone. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy and a girl who are neighbours for twenty years and only become friends on the day one of them moves away.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Gray and a girl named Ren lived next door to each other from the ages of four to twenty-four and had never become friends, being in different schools, then different social circles, then different schedules, until the morning of Gray's moving day when they were both in the street at six in the morning and Ren helped him carry a box to the van and they talked for two hours and discovered they had been missing, for twenty years, a friendship that would have cost them nothing to have. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy and a girl who are assigned to write the same story for school and produce two entirely different versions that are both correct.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Arc and a girl named Noa were each asked to write a story based on the same prompt and produced, independently, two stories so different in their approach and so similar in their quality that the teacher read both aloud without comment and asked the class which was better and the class could not agree, and the teacher said that was exactly the point, and Arc and Noa looked at each other across the room and for the first time found the other person genuinely interesting. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy and a girl who discover a hidden room in the school library and what they find inside it.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Miro and a girl named Zoe both stayed late in the school library on the same afternoon and discovered, behind a bookshelf that neither had noticed was not flush with the wall, a small room containing a desk, a lamp, a chair, and forty years of notebooks belonging to a previous librarian who had been writing an account of everything interesting that had ever happened in the school. They read it in shifts over the following weeks and learned that the school had been considerably more interesting than its current reputation suggested, and they wrote a sequel volume together and left it on the same desk for whoever came next. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy and a girl who are both running away from home and meet on the road.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Sev and a girl named Paz were both walking along the same road at the same time in the same direction with the same expression of determined uncertainty that people have when they have made a decision they are not entirely sure about yet. They walked in parallel for an hour before acknowledging each other, and then talked through the remainder of the afternoon, and by dusk had established that each had a better reason to go home than to continue and that the other person had been the first to say so, which made it easier to hear. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy and a girl who are apprenticed to the same master and compete until they realise they are being trained as a pair.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Roo and a girl named Dara were both apprenticed to the same old craftsman who set them tasks that seemed to be in competition but were, on closer inspection, always tasks that could only be judged successful together: the lock Roo made required the key Dara made, the map Dara drew was only useful with the compass Roo built, and the bridge they each built half of met in the middle on the day the master said nothing except good, which they eventually understood was the highest assessment available. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy and a girl who are both keeping the same secret and discover this about each other at the worst possible moment.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Ash and a girl named Calla had both been keeping the same secret for three years — that the old mill at the edge of town was not, as everyone believed, empty, but contained a family of displaced people who had been sheltering there quietly — and discovered each other's knowledge simultaneously when they both arrived on the same evening with food and found the other person already there. The moment of mutual recognition was followed by the practical question of what to do next, which they solved better together than either had been managing alone. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy and a girl who swap houses for a week and discover something about themselves from living in each other's space.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Pol and a girl named Ivy swapped houses for a week as part of a school exchange project and found that the other person's home, with its different rhythms and different sounds and different quality of morning light, revealed something about their own home they could not have seen from inside it. Pol found that Ivy's quieter house made his own house's noise feel like a form of love he had been taking for granted. Ivy found that Pol's busier house made her own house's stillness feel like a choice rather than an absence. Both of them went home differently. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy and a girl who are the only two people who believe in something the rest of the village has stopped believing in.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Eli and a girl named Bea were the last two people in a village who still left a small dish of milk out on the doorstep on midsummer night for the old custom's sake, everyone else having decided the practice was too old-fashioned to maintain. They did not know about each other until they compared notes at school one September and found the coincidence interesting. They kept leaving the milk and never found out whether it was collected by the thing the custom was meant for or by a neighbourhood cat, and agreed that both possibilities were acceptable. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy and a girl who are both invisible in a crowd and find each other because of it.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Fen and a girl named Sol were both the kind of people who went unnoticed in any gathering — not unpleasant, simply below the threshold at which groups tended to direct their attention — and they found each other at a party where both were standing at the edge of the room watching the same interaction in the middle of it with the same expression of mild detached interest. They talked for the rest of the evening without anyone noticing they had been there, which suited both of them exactly. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy and a girl who are given opposite advice by the same person and both follow it to the same place.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Will and a girl named Rue each separately asked an old woman on the crossroads road for advice about which way to go, and the old woman told each of them to take the road the other had come from, which sent them in opposite directions that curved, as roads in that country did, back toward the same village from different angles, where they arrived at sunset from east and west respectively and recognised each other as the strangers they had passed on the road that morning. The old woman was not there when they went back to ask about it. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy and a girl who are rivals in every competition except the one that matters.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Cas and a girl named Mira competed in every academic, athletic, and creative contest their school offered for eight years and kept a careful mental tally that neither would acknowledge aloud, and when a genuine crisis arrived — a younger child lost in the forest at the edge of town — they both ran toward it at the same time without thinking about the score and found the child and came back and did not add it to either tally, which was the first thing they had not competed over in eight years, and it changed the nature of the tally without either of them deciding to change it. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy and a girl who make a map of their neighbourhood together and discover it is larger than either of them knew.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Bram and a girl named Sela spent a summer making a map of their neighbourhood as a project and found, in the process of walking every street and alley and noting what was there, that the neighbourhood contained significantly more than either of them had known: a courtyard garden behind a door they had always assumed was a wall, a stream that had been built over but could still be heard if you stood at the right spot on the right street, and four different people who had been feeding the same cat for years from different households, each believing the cat was theirs. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who spends every Saturday helping his father restore an old car and what the project teaches him about the man.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Eli spent every Saturday of his twelfth year in the garage helping his father restore a car that had not run since before the boy was born. His father was not a talkative man at any other time but in the garage, with his hands in the engine, he talked continuously — about the car's history, about the summer he had driven it across the country, about a friend who had sat in the passenger seat who the boy had never heard mentioned before. The car ran again in October. The conversations, Eli understood later, had been the real project. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who discovers an abandoned car in the woods and the story he imagines for it.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Miro found a car deep in the woods behind his house, so overgrown that the trees had grown through the open windows and the seats were upholstered in moss. There was no road nearby that could explain how it had arrived. He visited it every week for a year, sitting in the driver's seat and constructing theories: it had driven itself there to retire, it had been parked by someone who never came back, it had always been there and the woods had simply grown up around it. He never found the answer and decided all three theories were probably true in different proportions. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who learns to drive from his grandfather on a long empty road and what else he learns along the way.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Tor learned to drive from his grandfather on the long straight road that ran through the farmland east of their town, and his grandfather, who rarely spoke at home, had opinions about driving that were really opinions about everything else: go as fast as conditions allow but know what the conditions are, keep enough distance to have time to think, and if you are not sure of your road, slow down and look. The boy passed his test three years later and thought of his grandfather at every junction. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who falls asleep in the back of a car on a long journey and wakes somewhere completely unexpected.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Cas fell asleep in the back seat of his family's car on what was supposed to be a four-hour journey and woke to find the car parked in a town he did not recognise, his parents having taken a wrong turn, corrected it, taken another, and eventually arrived somewhere entirely unplanned that turned out to have an excellent bakery and a small museum about a local shipwreck that Cas found more interesting than the original destination. He always slept on long journeys after that. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who builds a soapbox car and races it against all expectation.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Fen built a soapbox car from plans he found in a library book and parts he collected over four months, and entered it in the hill race expecting to come last, which was a reasonable expectation given that several competitors had been building for years. He did not come last. He came third, which he attributed to the aerodynamics he had read about but which his father attributed to the fact that Fen had built the thing himself and therefore understood every part of it when something went wrong on the second bend and he knew exactly what to do about it. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy and his mother's old car that becomes the most important object in his life after she is gone.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Dom inherited his mother's old hatchback when she died and found, over the first year, that he could not bring himself to change anything about it: the radio presets stayed on her stations, the air freshener she had hung from the mirror stayed until it had no smell left, and the parking permit from a hospital she had visited often stayed on the windscreen long after it expired. The car was eleven years old and not especially reliable and he drove it for another nine years before it finally could not be repaired, and he sat in it one last time in the garage before it was taken. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who washes cars for the whole street every weekend and what he notices from that particular vantage point.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Bram washed cars for twelve families on his street every Saturday for three years and noticed, from that particular proximity to people's vehicles, a great deal about how each family was doing: the family whose car was always immaculate was the one where things were tightly controlled, the family whose car was full of muddy sports kit and empty water bottles was the one having the most fun, and the family whose car had not been driven in three weeks was the one going through something nobody was talking about yet. He never said any of this out loud. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who names every car he has ever ridden in and the names he chooses.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Arc named every car he ever rode in: his grandmother's car was Margaret, the school minibus was the Reluctant One, the taxi that took them to the hospital the night his sister was born was the Blue Ambassador, and the car his father bought second-hand the summer Arc was ten was Gerald, for no reason he could explain. He kept a list in the back of a notebook and could recall the names of thirty-seven vehicles by the time he was grown, each name carrying with it the specific memory of where the car had taken him and why. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who is convinced the old car in his grandfather's barn is alive.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Sol was entirely certain that the old car in his grandfather's barn was alive, though he could not have explained this except by saying it felt warm in a way that metal left undriven should not, and that when he sat in the driver's seat it seemed to settle slightly, the way a large animal might settle when it recognised a familiar presence. His grandfather said nothing either way, which was his policy on things he could not explain, and when the grandfather died and the barn passed to Sol's family the car was the first thing Sol asked about. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who draws cars obsessively and grows up to design them.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Roo drew cars on every available surface from the age of four: in the margins of schoolbooks, on the backs of receipts his mother emptied from her pockets, on the condensation on cold windows, on the sand at the beach. By the time he was seven he had a system of notation for indicating speed and weight and aerodynamic efficiency. By the time he was twenty-three he was working in a design studio whose director had noticed that his sketches moved differently from other designers' — as though the cars on paper were already in motion. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who takes a long road trip with his uncle and discovers they have more in common than anyone in the family had noticed.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Gray spent two weeks in a car with his uncle Ash driving to a distant coast and back, which had been arranged partly as a holiday and partly because the family thought the two of them, who rarely spent time together, might benefit from extended proximity. They were right in a way none of them had specifically anticipated: both of them preferred silence to conversation, both found the movement of a car through landscape more restful than sitting still, and both had been reading the same three authors for years without knowing it. They talked a great deal, eventually. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who fixes a stranger's broken-down car and is given something unexpected in return.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Wick who had spent two summers learning basic mechanics from a neighbour stopped on the road when he saw an elderly woman standing beside a car with its bonnet up, looking at the engine with the resigned expression of someone who knows they do not know what they are looking at. The problem was a loose connection he fixed in ten minutes. The woman, who turned out to be a retired engineer, gave him her card and said if he was serious about mechanics he should call her, and the mentorship that followed was considerably more valuable than any payment he might have accepted. And they all lived happily ever after.
