instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who sleeps in a car during a family camping trip and finds it the best night's sleep of his life.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Sev declined the tent during a family camping trip and slept in the back of the car instead, which he had chosen for practical reasons — it had rained — and found, to his surprise, that the enclosed space, the sound of rain on the roof, and the particular quality of being both inside and outside at once produced the most complete sleep he had experienced in months. He told no one this at the time and spent years afterward trying to recreate the conditions with various degrees of success and one notable failure involving a hammock in a car park. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who starts a small business cleaning and valeting cars and what the work teaches him about people.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Pol started cleaning cars at thirteen with a bucket and a sponge and expanded to interior valeting by fifteen, and found that the inside of a car was among the most honest spaces a person owned: it contained what they actually used, what they actually listened to, what they actually ate when no one was watching, and the evidence of where they actually went. He never mentioned any of this to his clients, who would not have hired him again if he had, but he found it an unexpectedly thorough education in the difference between how people presented themselves and how they actually lived. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who wins a model car in a competition and spends years trying to find a car that matches it.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Eli won a model of a vintage car in a school raffle and became, in the following years, mildly obsessed with the real version — its history, how many had been made, where the surviving ones were. He never bought one, not having the money, but he saw one in a museum at twenty-six and stood in front of it for a long time comparing it to the model he still kept on his shelf, and found the real thing smaller than he had imagined and more beautiful than the model had suggested, which he thought was probably the right proportion of expectation to reality. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who delivers things by car across a small island and knows every road and every household on it.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Dov delivered goods across a small island for four years after school and on weekends, covering every road the island had in a rotation that took three days to complete, and came to know the island more completely than anyone he had met: which households received deliveries on which days, which roads flooded in autumn, which turn gave the best view of the harbour at the exact right moment, and which elderly resident would come to the door before he had finished knocking because she had been watching from the window since she heard the engine. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who paints a mural of cars on the wall of an abandoned garage and what happens to the wall.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Rue spent three weekends painting cars on the wall of an abandoned garage on his street — not realistic cars but the idea of cars, all colour and movement and implied speed — and the mural was discussed by the neighbours, photographed by people walking past, and eventually designated a local heritage feature before the garage was demolished, at which point a section of the wall was carefully cut out and moved to the town museum, where it was described in the label as an unknown local artist, which was accurate in the sense that Rue had never told anyone. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy whose father works night shifts as a taxi driver and what the boy learns from the stories his father brings home.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Ash grew up on the stories his father brought home from night taxi shifts: the woman who sang the entire journey, the man who had missed his daughter's birth and needed to talk about it, the couple who argued in whispers and then held hands when they thought the driver was not watching. His father said the car at night was a confessional and he had heard more honest things in it than anywhere else. Ash grew up to be a therapist and said his father had been a better training than any course he had taken. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who finds an old road atlas in his grandfather's car and uses it to plan a journey the atlas makes possible.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Bex found an old road atlas in the glovebox of his late grandfather's car, annotated in his grandfather's handwriting with restaurants, viewpoints, and comments like worth the detour and go in summer and closed Mondays. He spent a summer driving every marked route in order, finding that some notes were decades old and no longer accurate and others were accurate in ways that suggested his grandfather had been right about things in general and about this road in particular. He added his own notes in pencil alongside the originals. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy and a very small car that is just barely big enough for the adventures they have together.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Will bought the smallest car available for the smallest amount of money he had saved over two years and drove it across six countries, which required ingenuity in the mountains, patience on motorways, and a willingness to be laughed at by other drivers that Will found he had in greater supply than expected. The car, which he named the Optimist, required repairs in four countries and made a noise on long downhill stretches that suggested it was thinking hard about the situation. It completed every journey he asked of it and he kept it until it could not be kept any longer, which was a long time. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who moves into a very old house and slowly learns to understand what it is trying to tell her.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Brix moved into a house that was three hundred years old and spent the first month thinking its sounds were simply age and the second month suspecting they were more specific than that. The house creaked differently depending on the weather, tapped differently depending on the hour, and settled in a pattern she eventually understood as a kind of calendar, marking the anniversaries of things she had no record of but which the house remembered. She stopped trying to fix the sounds and started writing them down instead, and the house, she felt, was satisfied. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who inherits a house that everyone else in the family has refused.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Ora inherited a house that had passed through five sets of hands in the family, each refusing it after a season and citing reasons that were always slightly different and always slightly vague. Ora went to look at it expecting to find something wrong and found instead a house with good bones and a garden in serious need of attention and a view from the upstairs window that made her sit on the floor for twenty minutes before she was ready to move. She accepted the house. No one in the family could explain why it had suited her when it had suited no one else. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who builds her own house from the ground up and what each room teaches her.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Fen spent four years building her own house on a plot of land she had bought with careful savings, and found that each room, built in sequence, taught her something specific: the foundations taught her patience, the walls taught her the difference between load-bearing and decorative, the roof taught her to finish what she had started before the weather made the decision for her, and the kitchen, the last room completed, taught her that the thing you most need in a home is a room where people can sit and eat something and stay a while. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who discovers her house has a room that appears only at certain times of year.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Sela discovered that her house had a room that only appeared in the three weeks around the winter solstice, visible between the linen cupboard and the bathroom in a space that at other times of year contained only wall. The room was small, warm, and contained a chair, a lamp, and a different book each year, always one she had not read and always one she needed. She told no one about it and visited it every December and returned the books carefully before the room disappeared, which they always had when it came back. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who paints every room of her house a different colour and the colours she chooses reveal something she did not know about herself.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Mira moved into her first own home and painted each room without a plan, choosing colour by instinct for each space: the kitchen a warm yellow, the bedroom a deep blue, the study a green so dark it was almost black, the bathroom a pale grey that changed in different lights. A friend who visited said the house looked exactly like her and she did not understand this until she stood in the hallway and looked at each room in turn and understood that she had been describing herself in colour without meaning to, which was the most honest self-portrait she had ever made. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who grows up in a house that is never quite finished and what the permanent incompleteness teaches her.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Ivy grew up in a house her father was always improving, so that there was always a wall with a patch waiting for paint, a room with bare floorboards, a doorway with temporary curtain instead of door. She grew up understanding home not as a finished state but as a continuous act of attention, and when she had her own house she kept one room always in the process of becoming something else, not from any practical need but because the presence of work in progress made the rest of the house feel lived in rather than merely occupied. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who rents the smallest house on the street and makes it the most noticed.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Rue rented the smallest house on a long street of larger ones and spent her first summer making the front of it extraordinary: climbing roses trained along the entire façade, a window box that changed with every season, a door painted a colour no one on the street had previously considered for a door. Neighbours who had walked the street for years started noticing the small house for the first time, and Rue said she had not done it to be noticed but because she could only see the front of her house from the street and she wanted to enjoy it when she came home. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who finds letters hidden in the walls of her house during renovation and pieces together the life of the person who wrote them.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Cas found seventeen letters hidden in the wall cavity of her house during a renovation, written across forty years by the same hand and never sent, addressed to a person who was never named. She read them in order and assembled from them a life: a young woman arriving in the town, a marriage that started well and became difficult, a garden she loved, a friendship that mattered more than it was ever acknowledged, and a final letter that said simply that the house had been good to her and she hoped it would be good to whoever came next. Cas planted the same flowers the letters described. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who refuses to leave a house her family is being forced to sell.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Dara refused to leave the house her family was being forced to sell, not from stubbornness but from a genuine inability to explain to anyone why leaving felt like something other than a practical inconvenience. She stayed an extra week while the paperwork was finalised and spent it walking every room and sitting in every window and pressing her hand against the walls that had held the sound of her whole childhood, and on the last morning she left a letter in the kitchen explaining to the new owners which floorboard creaked and what the garden needed in spring and that she hoped the house would like them. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who fixes houses for other people but cannot fix what is wrong with her own.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Sol repaired houses for a living — replastering walls, re-hanging doors, rebuilding porches — and was excellent at diagnosing what was structurally wrong with any building she walked into. Her own house was another matter: the damp patch in the corner of her bedroom that she had been meaning to address for three years, the gate that she had propped shut with a brick, the kitchen shelf she had been meaning to rehang since it first sagged. She understood, when a friend pointed this out, that she had been too tired to care for her own things in the same way she cared for other people's, and she fixed the shelf that evening. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who moves into a new house and is welcomed by all the neighbourhood except one.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Wren moved into a house on a street where every neighbour brought something in the first week: a plant, a pie, directions to the best shops. Every neighbour except one, a woman at the far end who watched from her window but did not come. Wren brought her a pie herself at the end of the month and the woman, who was old and could no longer easily walk the street, said she had been waiting to see what kind of person would come before deciding. Wren was apparently the right kind. They became friends of the particular kind that forms between people separated by fifty years who recognise something in each other regardless. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who grows up in a house full of books and what happens the day she runs out of shelf space.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Pia grew up in a house where every wall had a shelf and every shelf had books, and the day she ran out of shelf space at the age of fourteen felt, briefly, like a genuine crisis. Her mother showed her instead how to make a double row, spine behind spine, and then how to stack horizontally, and then how to put books in the bathroom and the kitchen and the stairs, and then said: eventually you will give books away, and that is not a loss but a way of sending them somewhere they will be read, which is all a book actually wants. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who is the only person in her family who can feel what the house needs.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Lara was the only person in her family who seemed to sense when the house needed something: she felt the damp before it showed on the walls, knew which window was rattling before anyone else heard it, and could tell by the quality of cold in a room in October that the boiler needed servicing. Her family accepted this without particularly understanding it, and her father called her the house's voice, which she thought was the other way around. She became a surveyor and said she had never been wrong about a building. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who turns her grandmother's abandoned house into something the whole village uses.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Bea inherited her grandmother's large old house that had stood empty for three years and spent six months cleaning and repairing it with help from anyone who volunteered, of whom there were more than she had expected. She opened the ground floor as a shared space — a room for meetings, a kitchen for cooking classes, a corner with books anyone could take and return or not — and found that the house, which had been too large for one person and too empty for the village, was exactly the right size for what it became. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who moves into a house that used to belong to a scientist and finds her experiments still running.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Zoe moved into a house that had belonged to a scientist who had died the previous year, and found in the cellar three experiments still running on automatic timers, including one that the scientist had set up forty years ago and not yet concluded. Zoe called the scientist's daughter, who came to look, and neither of them knew what the experiment was for or what result it was approaching, but both agreed it would be wrong to stop it before it finished, so Zoe kept it running for another two years until the timer stopped on its own and the result, whatever it was, was apparently complete. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who is afraid of her own house at night and the summer she overcomes it.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Noa was afraid of her house at night, not of anything specific but of the house's nighttime self, which felt like a different place from the house she knew by day. She spent a summer systematically learning the night house: sitting in the kitchen at midnight until it felt ordinary, walking every room in the dark until she knew it by touch, lying on the living room floor listening to the sounds until she could name each one. By September the night house and the day house were the same house and she was not afraid of either. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who builds a miniature version of every house she has ever lived in.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Cass had moved eleven times by the age of twenty and built, after each move, a small cardboard model of the house she had just left, accurate in its proportions and particular in its details — the colour of each room, the view from the important window, the specific shape of the garden if there had been one. The collection sat on a shelf in every new place she arrived, a row of former homes, and she said looking at them reminded her that she had made each one work, which meant she could make the new one work too. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who reads the history of her house and discovers one of her ancestors lived in it.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Ren researched the history of her family's house for a school project and found, in the local archive, a record from a hundred and forty years before of a girl with the same surname living at the same address. The record was brief — a school roll, a census entry, a marriage record — but enough to establish that a version of her own family had occupied the same rooms, looked out the same windows, and walked the same stairs for a hundred years before her. She left a note in the archive in case anyone else came looking. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who makes every house she lives in smell the same.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Ivy moved frequently for her work and brought with her, to every new place, the same small set of things specifically chosen for their smell: a candle, a soap, a particular wood polish, and dried flowers from a variety her mother had grown. Within a week of arriving anywhere she had made the air in the house the same specific mixture of scents, and the house, whatever it actually was, smelled like home, which she had found was half of what made it one. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who leaves a mark in every room of every house she lives in and what the marks are.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Gray left a mark in every house she lived in, always in the same place — the inside of the highest cupboard in the kitchen — and always the same mark: her name and the year in pencil, small enough to miss unless you were looking. She had been doing this since she was seven in a house she was too young to own and too young to know she would miss, and by the time she was forty she had left her name in nine houses and thought sometimes of the people who lived in them now, who might someday open the highest cupboard and wonder who had been there first. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who talks to his house every morning and the morning the house talks back.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Fen had the habit of saying good morning to his house when he came downstairs, addressing it generally the way one might address a room full of people, and the house never responded except in the ordinary ways a house responds: a creak of approval, a draft of acknowledgment, a radiator ticking in what he chose to interpret as agreement. On the morning of his thirteenth birthday the house said good morning back, clearly and once, in a voice that sounded like the sound a house makes in a strong wind but arranged into words. He said good morning again. It did not speak again. But he never stopped saying it first. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who is the only one who can find things in a famously disorganised house.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Cas lived in a house so thoroughly disordered that visiting adults left looking slightly dazed, but Cas knew exactly where everything was, not because he had organised it but because he had learned the house's own logic, which was not alphabetical or categorical but associative and biographical: the scissors were near the birthday cards because his mother always wrapped presents in the kitchen, and the good pen was in the bathroom because his father read correspondence in the bath. He became the family's universal locator service and charged nothing but occasionally a biscuit. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who builds a second house inside his house and lives in both.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Miro spent a winter constructing a small house inside his bedroom, framed with timber offcuts and roofed with a sheet of plywood, with a door low enough to require ducking and a window cut to look out at his actual window. He slept in the inner house when he wanted to think and in his bed when he wanted to sleep and the distinction between the two became, over months, clearer and more useful than he had expected. His mother left it alone and only asked that he keep both houses tidy, which he did. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who inherits a house full of clocks and the clock he discovers is not measuring time.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Sol inherited his great-uncle's house and found every room contained clocks: wall clocks, mantel clocks, grandfather clocks, pocket watches in cases, and one clock in the cellar that ticked differently from all the others and whose face, on close inspection, had no numbers but instead small symbols he did not recognise. He spent a year learning what it measured and concluded, without being entirely certain, that it was measuring how much of a thing remained rather than how much had passed, which he found more useful than any clock he had previously owned. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who discovers a previous occupant of his house was exactly his age when something remarkable happened there.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Gray found in the local archive a newspaper account of something that had happened in his house seventy years before, involving a boy of exactly his own age who had, apparently, single-handedly prevented a fire from reaching the rest of the street. He visited the archive three more times to be sure of the details and then walked home and stood in the hallway thinking about the boy who had stood in the same hallway at the same age and done something worth recording, and found the house felt different after that, heavier in a way he did not mind. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who is afraid to go into one specific room of his house and the summer he finally does.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Eli had never gone into the back room of the house, the one with the door that swelled shut in winter and stood slightly open in summer, the one that smelled of something old and specific that he had no name for. At twelve, in August, he pushed the door fully open and went in and found a room full of his grandfather's things, stored there after the grandfather died before the boy was old enough to remember him: books, tools, a jacket on a hook, a pair of boots. He sat in the chair for an hour and then came back the next day, and the day after, all summer. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who maps every inch of his house and finds a discrepancy between the map and the house.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Arc spent three weeks making an exact floor plan of his house, measuring every wall and corridor and cupboard with a tape measure and recording everything to scale, and found when he was finished that the plan contained less space than the house appeared to from the outside by a margin of approximately two metres in the north wall. He checked his measurements four times and they were correct. He never found the source of the discrepancy and concluded that it was probably nothing, which is what you conclude about things you cannot explain in your own house when you still have to sleep there. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy whose house is the neighbourhood meeting point and what he learns from always being the host.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Dom grew up in the house that all the neighbourhood children came to, the one with the big kitchen and the tolerant parents and the garden with space for complicated games, and he learned from always being the host rather than the guest a particular set of skills: how to make people comfortable in your space without losing the space yourself, how to end an evening without it feeling like an eviction, and how to be glad when people leave as well as when they arrive. He was an excellent host his whole life and said the house had trained him. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who finds a door in his house that was not there the previous week.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Bram found a door in the upstairs hallway on a Tuesday that he was certain had not been there on the Monday. It was a small door, painted the same colour as the wall, with a brass handle that was warm to the touch. He opened it and found a narrow staircase leading up to a room that looked out over the street from an angle that should not have been possible from that part of the house. He went up every Tuesday for the following year and on the Tuesday after that the door was gone, and the wall was exactly as it had always been. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who repaints his family's old house and finds messages from the past under every layer.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Rue helped his father repaint the house over a summer and found, as each room was stripped back, evidence of the house's previous lives: a child's height marks on a door frame going back forty years, a pencilled shopping list from 1962, a set of wallpaper beneath the plaster printed with a pattern he found in a book on interior design as having been fashionable for exactly six years in the mid-century. He photographed everything before covering it again and put the photographs in a folder labelled what was here, which he kept. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who learns to cook in a kitchen that seems to have opinions about what should be made in it.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Tor began teaching himself to cook at eleven and found that the kitchen in his grandmother's house, where he spent his summers, seemed to have preferences: the bread always rose better when he used the old ceramic bowl than the metal one, the soup always tasted better when he used the cast iron pot regardless of the recipe, and there was a particular spot on the worktop, next to the window, where pastry handled itself with unusual willingness. He asked his grandmother about this and she said simply that a good kitchen knows what it is for. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who grows up in a series of houses and treats each one as a different chapter.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Sev moved seven times before he was sixteen and developed a habit of treating each house as its own complete thing: he learned each one's specific sounds and smells and light, and when they moved he let the previous house go cleanly rather than trying to carry it into the new one. His friends said he adapted too easily and he said he had simply learned that a house was a chapter and not the whole book, and that the book was something you carried in yourself rather than in any specific address. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who convinces his family to open one room of their house to strangers who need shelter.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Pol suggested at the age of ten that the spare room of their house, which contained boxes and an unused treadmill and served no purpose anyone could clearly articulate, could be used by people who needed somewhere to stay, and argued his case over three dinners with a thoroughness that surprised his parents into agreement. The room was used over the following five years by eleven people in various temporary difficulties and the family found that having the room in use felt better than having it empty, which none of them had expected and all of them agreed on. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who lives in a lighthouse and treats the light as a member of the household.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Dov grew up in a lighthouse with his father and treated the light as the third member of the household: it had a schedule, it had moods depending on the weather, and it required tending in a way that was not so different from anything else that lived in the house. He talked to it the way he talked to the cat, which was not expecting a response but was not quite not expecting one either, and when the automation came and the light no longer needed a keeper he felt its absence the way you feel the absence of someone who had always been reliably there. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who fixes everything that breaks in his house and the one thing he cannot fix.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Wick had fixed everything that broke in his family's house from the age of nine: a dripping tap, a stuck drawer, a boiler that needed a specific sequence of persuasion to restart. His parents called him the house doctor and he found the work satisfying in the direct way of things that were wrong and then right. The one thing he could not fix was a crack in the plaster in the kitchen that reappeared within a week of every repair, always the same crack, always in the same place, as though the house were insisting on keeping at least one visible record of something. He eventually stopped repairing it and learned to leave it alone. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who spends a summer alone in the family house and discovers what the house is like without the family in it.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Ash spent a month alone in the family house while his parents were caring for a sick relative in another city, and discovered that the house was a completely different place without the family inside it: quieter in expected ways and louder in unexpected ones, the sounds of the building itself more audible without the overlay of family life, the rooms more specifically themselves. He came to know the house differently that month than he had in eleven years of living in it with other people, and when the family returned he was briefly territorial in a way he did not know how to explain. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who grows sunflowers so tall they reach the upstairs windows and what his neighbours think.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Rue planted sunflowers along the side of his house in April and tended them with a thoroughness that surprised even him, and by late August they had reached the upstairs windows and one had grown tall enough to look in at his bedroom, which he found more companionable than alarming. The neighbours were divided: three found them magnificent, two found them excessive, and one found them personally offensive in a way she could not fully explain. Rue thanked the three, heard the two, and brought the one a jar of sunflower seeds from the heads at the end of the season, which did not resolve the matter but moved it to a different register. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who reads the same book in every room of his house to see how the rooms change it.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Eli read the same novel eleven times in eleven different locations around his house — the kitchen, the bathroom, the landing, the garden in good weather — and kept notes on how the book felt different depending on where he sat to read it, which rooms made it funnier and which made it sadder and which made the ending feel inevitable and which made it feel like a surprise. He showed his notes to his English teacher, who said this was either a very interesting experiment or a very elaborate way of avoiding reading something else. Both things were true. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who leaves his house for the first time in a year and what the world looks like from that distance.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Cob spent a year mostly at home following an illness that made the outside world difficult, and the house became, in that year, more thoroughly known to him than any place he had ever been: he knew the quality of light in every room at every hour, knew which floorboards were warmest in winter, knew the particular silence of three in the morning. When he finally walked outside again in spring the world was bright and loud and various in a way he had forgotten it was, and his house, seen from the street, looked smaller than he remembered and more specific than he had noticed before he had spent a year inside it. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who decides to say goodbye to his childhood house properly before his family moves.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Gray spent the last three days before his family's move saying goodbye to his house properly, which meant visiting every room at each time of day, sitting in every windowseat, lying on his back in the garden at night to see the sky from the specific angle the garden permitted, and eating breakfast at the kitchen table one last time on the morning of the moving lorry. He was thirteen and felt this was the right amount of ceremony for something that deserved ceremony, and he carried the house with him afterward not as sadness but as a place he knew he had not left carelessly. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a house and the oak tree that grew up beside it across three centuries, each shaping the other.
tale: Once upon a time, a house was built beside a young oak that the builder planted the same week he laid the foundations, intending them to grow together, and they did: the tree's roots found the house's drainage and followed it, the house's east wall stayed cooler because of the tree's shade and lasted longer because of it, and the tree grew around a nail someone had driven into its bark in 1840 and incorporated it so completely that the nail became simply part of the tree. By the third century the house leaned slightly toward the tree and the tree leaned slightly toward the house and neither was structurally the worse for it. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a family who will not cut down the tree growing through their house and the tree that understands this.
tale: Once upon a time, a family lived in a house through which a fig tree had grown, entering through the kitchen floor and exiting through the roof, having arrived as a seedling through a crack before any of them were born. Visitors suggested it should be removed. The family said it was a structural element by now, which was true, and also that they preferred it, which was more true. The tree fruited every August directly over the kitchen table, which the family considered convenient, and dropped leaves in October, which they swept up without complaint. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a girl who grows up between a house and a tree and can't decide which she belongs to.
tale: Once upon a time, a girl named Bea spent her childhood divided between the house, which was warm and full of people and smelled of bread, and the oak at the bottom of the garden, which was cold in the mornings and quiet at all times and smelled of bark and old weather. She slept in the house and thought in the tree and brought the thinking back inside, and when she was grown she built herself a house with a tree inside it, which was impractical and precisely right. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a tree that outlives every house built around it and the one house that finally lasts as long as the tree.
tale: Once upon a time, a yew tree that was already old when the first house was built beside it watched four houses rise and fall around it over four hundred years, each one eventually taken by fire or flood or simply by time. The fifth house was built differently, with materials chosen not for cost but for longevity, by a builder who had studied the previous four and understood what each had failed at. The tree, when the builder laid the last stone, made no particular sign that it approved, but it had been waiting long enough that approval seemed beside the point. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a house that is warm on one side because of a tree and cold on the other and what each side of the house becomes.
tale: Once upon a time, a house had a large chestnut tree on its western side that kept the afternoon sun off those rooms entirely, and the household arranged itself accordingly: the shaded rooms became the study and the bedroom and the room where things were stored, while the eastern rooms, bright and warm until noon, became the kitchen and the sitting room and the room where guests were received. The division felt natural to everyone who lived there and they attributed it to preference, never quite noticing that it had been decided for them by a tree. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a man who plants a tree the day he builds his house and what the tree and the house look like at the end of his life.
tale: Once upon a time, a man named Dom built a house at thirty and planted a rowan tree at the same time in the garden, and tended both across sixty years. By the time he was ninety the house had been repainted twice, had a new roof, and had absorbed four extensions; the rowan was taller than the house, broader, and had seeded two daughter trees at the garden's edge that had since grown half as tall as their parent. He sat on the step in his last autumn and looked at both and felt that the house had been the more deliberate project and the tree the more successful one. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a house built in the branches of a tree and the people who live in it and have never touched the ground.
tale: Once upon a time, a family built their house in the branches of an enormous plane tree that had been growing for two hundred years and had branches the diameter of ordinary trees and the height of a four-storey building. Three generations lived there and did touch the ground, of course — they had to, to work and trade and school — but the house in the branches was where they came home to, and coming home meant climbing, and the children who grew up there were known throughout the valley for their steadiness at heights and their habit of always checking, before settling, that the thing they sat on was properly supported. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a tree that grows fruit which, when eaten inside a particular house, tastes of a different season.
tale: Once upon a time, a pear tree grew beside a cottage in such precise alignment with the kitchen window that the pears dropped directly onto the sill each September, and the family who lived there had noticed over generations that eating the pears inside the kitchen tasted of late spring while eating them outside tasted simply of pear. No one could explain this. The mother said it was the angle of the light through the old glass. The grandmother said the house remembered when the tree flowered. The children did not care about the explanation and ate most of the pears outside anyway, which seemed to taste better for being ungoverned. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a tree that was planted when the house was built and that will die when the house is demolished.
tale: Once upon a time, a silver birch was planted the same day the foundations of a house were poured, by the same hands, and the two grew together in a way the neighbourhood found pleasant and the local naturalist found interesting. When the house was sold and the buyer announced plans to demolish and rebuild, the silver birch dropped all its leaves overnight in July, which birches do not do in July. It was noted. The buyer changed his plans. He renovated instead, and the birch leafed again within the week, which the naturalist described in a paper and the buyer described to guests for the rest of his life. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a hollow tree beside a house that becomes the neighbourhood's most important meeting place.
tale: Once upon a time, a hollow oak beside a corner house became, by gradual and unplanned agreement, the place where people in the neighbourhood left things for each other: a bag of apples from a surplus harvest, a note about a lost cat, a book someone had finished, a key that had been found on the pavement. The practice began with one family and spread because the hollow was convenient and the oak was old enough to seem permanent in a way that letterboxes were not. No one organised it and no one needed to and it worked for thirty years. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a house and a tree that have been drawing closer to each other for a hundred years.
tale: Once upon a time, a pine tree and a stone house stood twelve metres apart when both were new, and a surveyor measuring the property a hundred years later found them eight metres apart, the pine having extended its root system toward the house's warmth and the house having been extended twice in the direction of the pine's shade. Each generation had chosen to grow toward the other without knowing the previous generation had done the same. The current owners found the gap between tree and wall was now narrow enough to stand in comfortably and called it the corridor, and used it as such. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a woman who buys a house specifically because of the tree in the garden.
tale: Once upon a time, a woman named Ora viewed seventeen houses in four months and chose the eighteenth specifically because of the walnut tree in the back garden, which was old enough to have a circumference she could not reach around and which produced a canopy in summer that covered most of the garden in a particular filtered green light she had been looking for without knowing she was looking for it. The estate agent noted she spent the visit mostly sitting under the tree rather than examining the house. The house was entirely adequate. She lived there happily for thirty years. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a tree that grows through the window of an empty house and fills it room by room.
tale: Once upon a time, a house stood empty for twenty years after its last occupant moved to a care home and could not be sold, and in the eighteenth year a sycamore seedling that had taken root on the sill of the broken kitchen window grew through the gap and began filling the house room by room, slowly and without urgency. When the estate was finally settled and the house was opened, the ground floor was entirely the tree's, the branches reaching every corner and the light coming through in pieces. The new owner did not remove it but built around it and called the ground floor the woodland room and charged accordingly. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who learns the history of his house by reading the rings of the tree that was planted with it.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Eli learned from a tree surgeon that the oak in his garden could be dated by its rings, and that a cross-section of any fallen branch would show each year's growth. A branch had fallen in the winter storm, and the boy and the surgeon counted back together and found the oak was exactly as old as the house, planted the same year according to the records. The rings showed drought years and wet years and one year of unusually wide growth that corresponded, Eli found in the local archive, to the year the town's water supply was first piped to that street. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a house that smells like the tree beside it and the people who come from far away to experience it.
tale: Once upon a time, a house beside an ancient linden tree had, for the three weeks of the linden's flowering each June, an interior smell that visitors described as impossible to recreate: cool and sweet and specific in a way that made people sit quietly for a while before talking. Word spread and people began arriving in June from a considerable distance to simply sit inside the house during the flowering, which the family permitted on the grounds that the linden had been doing it for longer than they had lived there and the smell was not theirs to own. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a tree whose fallen leaves form a different pattern on the roof of the house each autumn.
tale: Once upon a time, a maple tree dropped its leaves onto the roof of the house beside it each October in a pattern that the family had been photographing from the attic window for fifteen years and which was never quite the same twice. They had catalogued the patterns by year and debated their meanings with the seriousness of people who understood they were probably making meaning rather than finding it, and continued anyway because the alternative — stopping photographing, abandoning the catalogue — felt like a loss they were not prepared to accept. The maple, for its part, simply dropped its leaves as it always had. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a house that was built around a tree so the tree could remain and what that decision cost and gave the builder.
tale: Once upon a time, a builder named Fen bought a plot of land containing a large ash tree and designed the house around it rather than removing it, which added cost and complexity and produced a house with an irregular footprint and a room with a tree trunk running through one corner. The buyers of the house paid more than expected because of it and said the trunk in the living room corner was the reason they had chosen the house over six others. Fen said the decision had been sentimental and impractical and entirely worth making and he built three more houses with trees inside them before he retired. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about an old tree that protects a house from every storm and the storm that finally tests both.
tale: Once upon a time, an elm tree stood windward of a farmhouse and had broken the force of every storm that had come from the north for a hundred and thirty years, the farmhouse records noting after each event that the tree had held. In the worst storm in living memory the elm lost two major branches and held. The farmhouse lost three roof tiles and held. In the morning the farmer came out and looked at the tree and the tree looked, to the extent a tree can look like anything, exactly like something that had been through a serious test and was still there, which was the most important thing. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a family that plants a tree for every child born in the house and what the garden looks like four generations later.
tale: Once upon a time, a family began the tradition of planting a tree for every child born in the house: apple for the first, cherry for the second, and so on through five siblings and four generations until the garden contained twenty-three trees of eleven varieties in no particular order but complete and specific to each person, the oldest as old as the house and the youngest planted six months ago. The garden was too full for a lawn but had instead a canopy that produced fruit across five months of the year and a shade in summer that kept the house cool in a way that no modern system could have replicated. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a house and a tree that are both considered beyond saving and the person who saves both.
tale: Once upon a time, a derelict house and the elm beside it were both considered beyond saving by the professionals who assessed them: the house too structurally compromised, the tree too diseased. A woman named Cass who had recently retired from a career in restoration disagreed and said she had seen worse, which was true, and spent three years working on both simultaneously, treating the elm's disease while repairing the house's foundations, finding as she worked that the two projects informed each other in ways she had not anticipated. Both survived. The house is lived in. The elm is flowering. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who climbs the same mountain every year on his birthday and what the mountain shows him each time.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Tor climbed the mountain behind his village every year on his birthday, starting at seven when he only reached the first ridge and extending further each year as he grew. The mountain showed him different things at different ages: at nine a hawk's nest, at twelve a spring he had not known existed, at fifteen a view of the coast he had not known was visible from that height, and at seventeen, on the clearest morning of his life, the outline of a further mountain he had not known was there, which gave him somewhere new to go. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who is convinced the mountain near his village is listening and the day he finds proof.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Eli had always believed the mountain at the edge of his village was listening, without being able to explain this except that sounds made near its base seemed to return slightly changed, as though considered before being given back. He spent three years testing this with different sounds at different times and different weather and on a still morning in October the mountain returned his own name to him in a register he did not recognise as his own voice, which was either an echo with unusual properties or something else, and he decided the distinction did not matter as much as the fact that it had happened. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who lives on a mountain and has never been to the valley and a boy from the valley who has never been to the mountain.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Arc who lived on the mountain and had never descended and a boy named Dom who lived in the valley and had never climbed met at the halfway point where a path crossed a stream, each having decided on the same morning that it was finally time. They spent an afternoon describing their respective worlds to each other with the precision of people reporting on something extraordinary, which each found the other's ordinary life to be, and went home with a doubled sense of the world that neither had expected to find at a midpoint. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who gets lost on a mountain and finds something that makes him glad he got lost.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Sev got genuinely, thoroughly lost on the mountain above his town during a walk he had done a dozen times before, the path having disappeared under new snow, and spent four hours finding his way down by a route he had never taken. The route passed through a valley he had not known existed, entirely hidden from the usual path, containing a frozen lake the colour of pale green glass and a stand of birch trees so old their bark had gone entirely black. He found his way home an hour before dark. He went back to the hidden valley every winter for the rest of his life. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who draws the same mountain every day for ten years and what changes and what stays the same.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Miro began drawing the mountain visible from his bedroom window at the age of eight and drew it every day for ten years, always from the same position, always at the same hour of the morning. Looking back through the drawings he found that the mountain itself had not changed at all but that everything around it had — the town in the foreground grew, a road appeared, a field became a car park — and that what had changed most was the quality of attention in the line, the earliest drawings enthusiastic and approximate and the later ones increasingly specific, as though he had spent ten years learning to actually see something he had always been looking at. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who learns to ride a bicycle after everyone has stopped believing he will manage it.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Cas had been trying to learn to ride a bicycle for two years, long enough that his family had quietly stopped mentioning it and his friends had long since moved on to other things, and on a Tuesday afternoon in October with nobody watching he simply got on the bicycle and rode it to the end of the lane and back, which was not miraculous except in the specific way that doing a thing you had been told by your own repeated failure was beyond you is always miraculous. He told no one that evening and told everyone the following morning. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy whose bicycle takes him a different route every day regardless of what he intends.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Rue owned a bicycle that was entirely reliable in every mechanical respect but which consistently delivered him somewhere other than his intended destination, always somewhere adjacent to where he had meant to go but specific in a way that felt deliberate: the long way past the bakery when he was hungry, past the library when he had a question he had not yet formed, past the house of the person he had been avoiding when he was finally ready to stop avoiding them. He stopped fighting it in the third month and started arriving early enough to allow for the detour. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who cycles across the country to deliver something important and what happens to him on the way.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Eli set off on his bicycle to deliver a letter his grandmother had written forty years ago to a person she had lost touch with and never stopped thinking about, and spent eleven days crossing the country, and arrived at the address on the envelope to find the person still there, now very old, who took the letter and read it at the kitchen table and said simply that she had wondered, and gave Eli a letter to take back, and he cycled home in nine days because he knew the roads better now. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy who repairs a bicycle he finds abandoned and what the bicycle turns out to mean to the person who lost it.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Dom found a bicycle locked to a railing on his street with a broken chain and a flat tyre and a lock whose owner had apparently lost the key, and over several weeks obtained permission from the council, freed it, and repaired it carefully with parts he bought with his own savings. When he posted a photograph in the local group a woman replied within the hour, her message so full of relief that he understood immediately the bicycle was not merely a bicycle. He delivered it to her door. She had not been able to afford to replace it and had been walking to work for four months. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a boy and his grandfather who go on a bicycle ride every Sunday and what the rides become over the years.
tale: Once upon a time, a boy named Bram began cycling with his grandfather every Sunday at the age of eight, initially a short loop around the park that took twenty minutes, and the loop expanded as Bram grew stronger and the grandfather grew slower, each year finding a new equilibrium, the distance shortening slightly in the grandfather's eighties and the pace adjusting again, until the last Sunday they cycled together when the grandfather was eighty-nine and they went just to the end of the road and back at walking pace, and Bram said that had been his favourite ride of all of them, which was true. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a child who can see what adults have forgotten and uses this to help them remember what matters.
tale: Once upon a time, a child named Sela could see, around every adult she met, a faint outline of who they had been before the world had made them cautious: the woman at the post office who had once wanted to paint, the man who drove the bus who had once wanted to sail, the teacher who had once collected beetles with the same absorption she now brought to marking. Sela did not tell people what she saw but she asked questions that pointed in the right directions, and the answers those questions produced surprised the people who gave them, and some of them went home and did something they had not done in years. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a child who is given one day to do exactly as they please and what they choose.
tale: Once upon a time, a child named Tor was given a single day by his parents to do entirely as he wished, with no schedule and no obligations, and spent the morning lying in the garden watching clouds, the afternoon building something from sticks that was not anything in particular, and the evening asking his grandmother questions until she fell asleep mid-answer. When his parents asked at bedtime whether the day had been everything he had hoped for he said yes without hesitation, and they found this more instructive about what their child actually needed than any amount of planning had been. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a child who asks a question no adult can answer and sets out to find the answer herself.
tale: Once upon a time, a child named Fen asked her parents why the sky was dark between the stars rather than bright, since there were so many stars, and her parents said they did not know and suggested she ask her teacher, who said it was a very good question, and her teacher suggested she ask the librarian, who found her a book that came close but did not entirely satisfy her, and she spent a year reading and asking and eventually understood that the question had a real answer involving the age of the universe and the speed of light, and that she had found it herself by following the question patiently wherever it went. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a child who befriends an elderly neighbour and what each gives the other.
tale: Once upon a time, a child named Bea began visiting the elderly man next door because he had a garden she could see from her window and she wanted to know what was growing in it. He told her, and showed her, and over two years taught her the names of everything in the garden and the conditions each plant preferred and the history of a few of them. She brought him, in return, a weekly account of what she had learned at school, which he received with the attention of someone who had been a long time without new information arriving at speed and with enthusiasm. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a child who collects small ordinary things and the museum she makes of them.
tale: Once upon a time, a child named Mira collected things that no one else considered worth keeping: a particularly smooth pebble, a bus ticket from a journey she had enjoyed, a piece of string in an unusual colour, a seed head from a plant she could not name, a button that had fallen from a coat belonging to someone she admired. She kept them in a wooden box with labels she wrote herself, and when her grandmother asked what the collection was for she said it was a museum of days, which her grandmother found so accurate that she asked if she might donate something to it, and the museum grew. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a child who plants a flower for every person she loves and tends the garden as a record of her life.
tale: Once upon a time, a child named Bea planted a flower for every person she loved, starting with a marigold for her mother and a sunflower for her grandfather and a small violet for the friend who had moved away but not been forgotten. The garden grew slowly and specifically across her childhood, each plant chosen to match something true about the person it represented, and when she was grown and people visited they said the garden felt unusually full of character for its size, which was exactly right since every plant in it was a person. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a child who discovers that a particular flower only blooms when she is nearby and nowhere else.
tale: Once upon a time, a child named Fen noticed that the pale blue flower growing in the corner of the school garden bloomed only on the days she visited it and was always closed when she did not, which she confirmed over a full term of careful observation before mentioning it to anyone. The teacher who came to verify the claim spent three weeks checking and confirmed the pattern without being able to explain it, and the flower was eventually written up in a small naturalist journal as an anomaly, which Fen found satisfying but less interesting than the fact itself. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a child who learns the language of flowers from an old book and begins leaving bouquets with messages for the people around her.
tale: Once upon a time, a child named Mira found a Victorian guide to the language of flowers in a second-hand shop and spent a month learning it, and began composing small bouquets for the people around her: red tulips for her father to say I love you directly, rosemary for her grandmother to say I remember you, white clover for the boy at school who needed to know someone wished him luck. Nobody recognised the language except her grandmother, who had learned it from her own grandmother, and who looked at the bouquet for a long time before looking at Mira with an expression that needed no translation. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a child who grows a flower from a seed she was told would never germinate.
tale: Once upon a time, a child named Dom was given a seed at a school fair that the stallholder said was almost certainly not viable, having been stored too long, and was probably best kept as a curiosity rather than planted. Dom planted it anyway in a pot on her windowsill, watered it every morning with the consistency of someone who had not yet learned to expect failure, and eleven weeks later a shoot appeared that became in time a flower no one in the family could identify, which they sent a photograph of to a botanist who wrote back with some excitement that it was a variety she had not seen growing outside a specialist collection in fifteen years. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a child who brings a dying garden back to life one flower at a time.
tale: Once upon a time, a child named Sol spent every afternoon of the summer at her great-aunt's house, which had a garden that had been neglected since the great-aunt's knees had made kneeling impossible, and began clearing and planting in a small section near the kitchen door that she could reach from the path. She asked her great-aunt what had grown there and planted accordingly, and the great-aunt, who had watched from the kitchen window, began coming to the door to give instructions, and then to sit in a chair on the path to give better instructions, and by late summer was kneeling again with a cushion, which she had said she would never manage, revising her opinion in the presence of sufficient reason. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a child who teaches her dog a trick nobody believes is possible and the afternoon they prove everyone wrong.
tale: Once upon a time, a child named Ora spent four months teaching her dog, a large and generally unhurried hound named Fig, to count to three by tapping his paw, and nobody in the family believed this was happening because Fig had the demeanour of a dog entirely uninterested in performance. On the afternoon of the school fair Ora entered the small animal talent competition and Fig tapped his paw three times on request in front of forty people with the expression of a dog doing a small favour for someone he was fond of. They won first prize. Fig accepted the applause with dignity and went to sleep immediately after. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a child who finds a lost dog and the search for its owner that changes both their lives.
tale: Once upon a time, a child named Sev found a small grey dog sitting outside the library in the rain with a collar but no tag, and took it home and spent two weeks making posters and posting photographs and knocking on doors in a widening circle around the library. The owner turned out to be an elderly man three streets away who had been too unwell to search himself and who wept quietly when Sev arrived at his door with the dog, and Sev visited every week after that, initially to check on the dog and eventually simply because the man had stories worth hearing and appreciated the company. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a child who is afraid of all dogs except one and what that one dog teaches her about fear.
tale: Once upon a time, a child named Mira was afraid of all dogs without exception until she was eight, when she spent a week at her cousin's house where there was a very old, very slow dog named Peg who moved through the world with such complete unhurriedness that Mira found herself sitting beside her on the third day without having decided to, and then on the fourth day resting her hand on the dog's warm flank, and on the fifth day understood that her fear had always been of sudden movement and unpredictability, and that Peg, who had run out of both, had simply removed the conditions that produced the fear. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a child and his dog who keep each other company through a long difficult year.
tale: Once upon a time, a child named Gray spent a year that was difficult in ways he could not fully explain to the adults around him in the company of his dog Moss, who required walking twice a day regardless of how Gray felt, which got him outside when he would not otherwise have gone, and who slept at the foot of his bed with a warmth and consistency that asked nothing of him, and who was, Gray said later, not a solution to anything but a steady presence that made everything slightly more survivable, which was more than enough. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a child who trains as a guide dog puppy walker and what the goodbye teaches her.
tale: Once upon a time, a child named Bea volunteered with her family to raise a guide dog puppy for its first year, socialising it and teaching it basic manners before it went on to formal training, and fell so completely in love with the puppy, a golden dog named Birch, that the goodbye at the end of the year was the hardest thing she had done. She cried for two days. Then she asked to raise the next puppy. She raised six dogs across her childhood and said each goodbye got harder and she kept doing it anyway, which she understood, eventually, as the point. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a child who gives away her favourite toy to someone who needs it more and what she receives in return.
tale: Once upon a time, a child named Bea had a stuffed rabbit she had loved since she was two, worn down to almost nothing, and on the day she saw a younger child at the hospital crying with nothing to hold she gave it over without much deliberation and walked home with empty hands that felt, after a while, lighter than they had in years. What she received in return was not immediately obvious and arrived slowly: a clearer sense of what she actually needed and what she had simply been keeping out of habit, which was a useful thing to know at seven and even more useful later. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a child who makes all his own toys and what the making teaches him that the toys themselves never could.
tale: Once upon a time, a child named Dom made all his own toys from whatever he could find: a boat from a piece of bark, a figure from twisted wire, a castle from a cardboard box that took four evenings and three failed attempts before the towers stayed upright. His parents offered to buy him things and he declined, not from any principle but because the making was the part he liked, and a finished toy that had not been made by him seemed to him to have skipped the best part. He grew up to be an engineer who said the cardboard castle had taught him more about structural failure than any textbook. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a child who discovers her grandmother's old toys in the attic and the afternoon they spend together because of it.
tale: Once upon a time, a child named Sela found a box in the attic containing her grandmother's childhood toys: a small tin car, a set of wooden blocks, a cloth doll with a repaired seam, and a spinning top that still spun. She brought the box downstairs and her grandmother, who had not seen these things in sixty years, sat with her on the floor for an entire afternoon picking each one up and remembering aloud, specifically and with great detail, the days attached to them. Sela listened without interrupting, which was the best thing she did that year, and remembered most of it. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a child who repairs broken toys for other children in the neighbourhood and the reputation this earns her.
tale: Once upon a time, a child named Ivy began repairing broken toys at nine, starting with her own and expanding to those brought by neighbours after word got out that she had a steady hand and better glue than most adults kept in the house. She charged nothing and asked only to know what had happened to each toy, which she recorded in a notebook alongside the repair, so that the notebook became over two years a catalogue of small accidents and enthusiasms and the particular ways children loved things hard enough to break them. The neighbourhood called her the toy doctor, which she accepted without false modesty. And they all lived happily ever after.

instruction: Write a fairy tale about a child who cannot bring himself to stop playing with a toy he has outgrown and what finally allows him to let it go.
tale: Once upon a time, a child named Eli was twelve and still kept a small wooden train on his desk that he had played with from the age of three, not because he still played with it but because he could not work out how to stop keeping it without losing something he could not name. He eventually understood, when his younger cousin visited and played with the train for an entire afternoon with the absorption he himself had once brought to it, that what he had been keeping was not the train but the memory of loving it that completely, and that the memory did not live in the object and would not leave with it. He gave it to his cousin. And they all lived happily ever after.
